crossReferences.connection: Psalm 106:30-31 -- 'But Phinehas stood up and intervened, and the plague was checked. This was credited to him as righteousness for endless generations.'

verses.text: Those who died by the plague were twenty-four thousand.
verses.explainer: The plague kills 24,000 people -- a devastating toll that underscores the severity of covenant betrayal.

crossReferences.reference: 1 Corinthians 10:8 -- Paul cites this event (with the number 23,000, possibly counting differently) as a warning to Christians against sexual immorality.
crossReferences.connection: 1 Corinthians 10:8 -- Paul cites this event (with the number 23,000, possibly counting differently) as a warning to Christians against sexual immorality.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about Phinehas' action.

verses.text: “Phinehas, the son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron the priest, has turned my wrath away from the children of Israel, in that he was jealous with my jealousy among them, so that I didn’t consume the children of Israel in my jealousy.
verses.explainer: God declares that Phinehas has turned God's anger away from Israel by being zealous with God's own zeal -- sharing God's jealousy for covenant faithfulness.

verses.text: Therefore say, ‘Behold, I give to him my covenant of peace.
verses.explainer: God grants Phinehas 'my covenant of peace' -- a paradoxical reward of peace for an act of violence.

verses.text: It shall be to him, and to his offspring after him, the covenant of an everlasting priesthood, because he was jealous for his God, and made atonement for the children of Israel.’”
verses.explainer: The covenant of a lasting priesthood is given to Phinehas and his descendants because he was zealous for his God and made atonement for Israel.

thenVsNow.then: Phinehas' priestly line continued through Israel's history. The Zadokite priests who served in Solomon's temple traced their lineage through Phinehas.
thenVsNow.now: The 'covenant of peace' given for an act of violence creates theological tension that requires careful interpretation. The narrative endorses Phinehas' motivation (zeal for God) within its specific historical context.

verses.text: Now the name of the man of Israel that was slain, who was slain with the Midianite woman, was Zimri, the son of Salu, a prince of a fathers’ house among the Simeonites.
verses.explainer: The Israelite man killed was Zimri son of Salu, a leader of a Simeonite family -- a prominent figure, not a marginal one.

verses.text: The name of the Midianite woman who was slain was Cozbi, the daughter of Zur. He was head of the people of a fathers’ house in Midian.
verses.explainer: The Midianite woman was Cozbi daughter of Zur, a tribal chief of a Midianite family -- also a person of high status.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord tells Moses to treat the Midianites as enemies.

verses.text: “Harass the Midianites, and strike them,
verses.explainer: Israel is to harass and defeat the Midianites for their role in the Peor incident.

verses.text: for they harassed you with their wiles, wherein they have deceived you in the matter of Peor, and in the incident regarding Cozbi, the daughter of the prince of Midian, their sister, who was slain on the day of the plague in the matter of Peor.”
verses.explainer: The Midianites treated Israel as enemies through deception at Peor, using Cozbi and the seduction strategy that Numbers 31:16 attributes to Balaam's counsel.

explainer.summary: After the plague, God commands a second census of all men twenty years and older who can serve in the army. The total is 601,730 -- slightly less than the first census (603,550). This census has a dual purpose: military readiness for conquering Canaan and determining land allotments by tribal size. The chapter emphasizes that the entire first generation has died except Joshua and Caleb, exactly as God had declared.
themes: The second generation replaces the first
God's judgment fulfilled -- the first generation has perished
Preparation for conquest and land distribution
Continuity of the twelve tribes despite massive mortality
Joshua and Caleb as sole survivors
explainer.contextInBook: This census mirrors the first census in chapter 1, forming a literary frame around the wilderness period. The first census counted those who left Egypt; the second counts those who will enter Canaan. Between the two censuses, an entire generation has died in the wilderness as God decreed.

wordStudies.significance: Land distribution is based on 'nachalah' -- inheritance. This is not temporary residence but permanent family possession. Each tribe's allotment is proportional to its census count, ensuring equity while maintaining tribal identity.

verses.text: After the plague, Yahweh spoke to Moses and to Eleazar the son of Aaron the priest, saying,
verses.explainer: After the plague, the Lord speaks to Moses and Eleazar son of Aaron the priest -- Aaron himself is now dead.

verses.text: “Take a census of all the congregation of the children of Israel, from twenty years old and upward, by their fathers’ houses, all who are able to go out to war in Israel.”
verses.explainer: God commands a census of the whole Israelite community by families -- all men twenty years or older who can serve in the army.

verses.text: Moses and Eleazar the priest spoke with them in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho, saying,
verses.explainer: Moses and Eleazar speak to the people on the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho.

verses.text:
“Take a census, from twenty years old and upward; as Yahweh commanded Moses and the children of Israel.”
These are those that came out of the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: The instruction: count the men twenty years or older, as the Lord commanded Moses. These are the Israelites who came out of Egypt.

verses.text: Reuben, the firstborn of Israel; the sons of Reuben: of Hanoch, the family of the Hanochites; of Pallu, the family of the Palluites;
verses.explainer: Reuben, the firstborn of Israel. The clans of Reuben are listed beginning with Hanoch.

verses.text: of Hezron, the family of the Hezronites; of Carmi, the family of the Carmites.
verses.explainer: The Palluites through Pallu.

verses.text: These are the families of the Reubenites; and those who were counted of them were forty-three thousand seven hundred thirty.
verses.explainer: These are the clans of Reuben, numbering 43,730.

thenVsNow.then: Reuben's total dropped from 46,500 in the first census to 43,730 -- a decline of about 6%. The firstborn tribe was shrinking.
thenVsNow.now: Reuben's decline reflects the biblical theme that firstborn status does not guarantee prominence. God's purposes often bypass natural expectations.

verses.text: The sons of Pallu: Eliab.
verses.explainer: Pallu's son was Eliab.

verses.text: The sons of Eliab: Nemuel, and Dathan, and Abiram. These are that Dathan and Abiram, who were called by the congregation, who rebelled against Moses and against Aaron in the company of Korah, when they rebelled against Yahweh,
verses.explainer: Eliab's sons were Nemuel, Dathan, and Abiram -- the Dathan and Abiram who joined Korah's rebellion against Moses and Aaron.

crossReferences.reference: Numbers 16 -- Dathan and Abiram were swallowed by the earth along with Korah. Their mention here connects the census to the rebellion narrative.
crossReferences.connection: Numbers 16 -- Dathan and Abiram were swallowed by the earth along with Korah. Their mention here connects the census to the rebellion narrative.

verses.text: and the earth opened its mouth, and swallowed them up together with Korah, when that company died; at the time the fire devoured two hundred fifty men, and they became a sign.
verses.explainer: The earth opened and swallowed them along with Korah when fire consumed the 250 men. They became a warning sign.

verses.text: Notwithstanding, the sons of Korah didn’t die.
verses.explainer: But the line of Korah did not die out -- his descendants survived.

verses.text: The sons of Simeon after their families: of Nemuel, the family of the Nemuelites; of Jamin, the family of the Jaminites; of Jachin, the family of the Jachinites;
verses.explainer: The clans of Simeon are listed.

verses.text: of Zerah, the family of the Zerahites; of Shaul, the family of the Shaulites.
verses.explainer: The Zerahites through Zerah.

verses.text: These are the families of the Simeonites, twenty-two thousand two hundred.
verses.explainer: These are the clans of Simeon: 22,200 -- the most dramatic decline of any tribe.

verses.text: The sons of Gad after their families: of Zephon, the family of the Zephonites; of Haggi, the family of the Haggites; of Shuni, the family of the Shunites;
verses.explainer: The clans of Gad are listed.

verses.text: of Ozni, the family of the Oznites; of Eri, the family of the Erites;
verses.explainer: The Oznites through Ozni.

verses.text: of Arod, the family of the Arodites; of Areli, the family of the Arelites.
verses.explainer: The Arodites and Arelites.

verses.text: These are the families of the sons of Gad according to those who were counted of them, forty thousand and five hundred.
verses.explainer: These are the clans of Gad: 40,500.

verses.text: The sons of Judah: Er and Onan. Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan.
verses.explainer: The sons of Judah: Er and Onan died in Canaan, according to Genesis.

verses.text: The sons of Judah after their families were: of Shelah, the family of the Shelanites; of Perez, the family of the Perezites; of Zerah, the family of the Zerahites.
verses.explainer: The clans of Judah through his remaining sons.

verses.text: The sons of Perez were: of Hezron, the family of the Hezronites; of Hamul, the family of the Hamulites.
verses.explainer: The clans through Perez: the Hezronites and Hamulites.

verses.text: These are the families of Judah according to those who were counted of them, seventy-six thousand five hundred.
verses.explainer: These are the clans of Judah: 76,500 -- the largest tribe, fulfilling Jacob's prophecy of Judah's preeminence.

crossReferences.reference: Genesis 49:8-12 -- Jacob prophesied that Judah would be praised and rule over his brothers. The tribe's numerical dominance confirms this trajectory.
crossReferences.connection: Genesis 49:8-12 -- Jacob prophesied that Judah would be praised and rule over his brothers. The tribe's numerical dominance confirms this trajectory.

verses.text: The sons of Issachar after their families: of Tola, the family of the Tolaites; of Puvah, the family of the Punites;
verses.explainer: The clans of Issachar are listed.

verses.text: of Jashub, the family of the Jashubites; of Shimron, the family of the Shimronites.
verses.explainer: The Jashubites and Shimronites.

verses.text: These are the families of Issachar according to those who were counted of them, sixty-four thousand three hundred.
verses.explainer: These are the clans of Issachar: 64,300.

verses.text: The sons of Zebulun after their families: of Sered, the family of the Seredites; of Elon, the family of the Elonites; of Jahleel, the family of the Jahleelites.
verses.explainer: The clans of Zebulun are listed.

verses.text: These are the families of the Zebulunites according to those who were counted of them, sixty thousand five hundred.
verses.explainer: The Sardites and Elonites.

verses.text: The sons of Joseph after their families: Manasseh and Ephraim.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Joseph by his sons Manasseh and Ephraim.

verses.text: The sons of Manasseh: of Machir, the family of the Machirites; and Machir became the father of Gilead; of Gilead, the family of the Gileadites.
verses.explainer: The clans of Manasseh: Makir fathered Gilead; from Gilead come the clans listed.

verses.text: These are the sons of Gilead: of Iezer, the family of the Iezerites; of Helek, the family of the Helekites;
verses.explainer: The Iezerites and Helekites through Gilead's sons.

verses.text: and Asriel, the family of the Asrielites; and Shechem, the family of the Shechemites;
verses.explainer: The Asrielites and Shechemites.

verses.text: and Shemida, the family of the Shemidaites; and Hepher, the family of the Hepherites.
verses.explainer: The Shemidaites and Hepherites.

verses.text: Zelophehad the son of Hepher had no sons, but daughters: and the names of the daughters of Zelophehad were Mahlah, and Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.
verses.explainer: Zelophehad son of Hepher had no sons, only daughters: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.

verses.text: These are the families of Manasseh. Those who were counted of them were fifty-two thousand seven hundred.
verses.explainer: These are the clans of Manasseh: 52,700.

verses.text: These are the sons of Ephraim after their families: of Shuthelah, the family of the Shuthelahites; of Becher, the family of the Becherites; of Tahan, the family of the Tahanites.
verses.explainer: The clans of Ephraim are listed.

verses.text: These are the sons of Shuthelah: of Eran, the family of the Eranites.
verses.explainer: The Shuthelahites and through Eran.

verses.text: These are the families of the sons of Ephraim according to those who were counted of them, thirty-two thousand five hundred. These are the sons of Joseph after their families.
verses.explainer: These are the clans of Ephraim: 32,500. These are the descendants of Joseph by their clans.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Ephraim's decline from 40,500 to 32,500 is notable because Jacob had prophesied that Ephraim would be greater than Manasseh (Genesis 48:19). In this census, Manasseh (52,700) significantly outnumbers Ephraim.

verses.text: The sons of Benjamin after their families: of Bela, the family of the Belaites; of Ashbel, the family of the Ashbelites; of Ahiram, the family of the Ahiramites;
verses.explainer: The clans of Benjamin are listed.

verses.text: of Shephupham, the family of the Shuphamites; of Hupham, the family of the Huphamites.
verses.explainer: The Shuphamites and Huphamites.

verses.text: The sons of Bela were Ard and Naaman: the family of the Ardites; of Naaman, the family of the Naamites.
verses.explainer: Bela's sons: Ard and Naaman.

verses.text: These are the sons of Benjamin after their families; and those who were counted of them were forty-five thousand six hundred.
verses.explainer: These are the clans of Benjamin: 45,600.

verses.text: These are the sons of Dan after their families: of Shuham, the family of the Shuhamites. These are the families of Dan after their families.
verses.explainer: The clans of Dan are listed: the Shuhamites.

verses.text: All the families of the Shuhamites, according to those who were counted of them, were sixty-four thousand four hundred.
verses.explainer: All the Shuhamite clans: 64,400 -- Dan is now the second largest tribe.

verses.text: The sons of Asher after their families: of Imnah, the family of the Imnites; of Ishvi, the family of the Ishvites; of Beriah, the family of the Berites.
verses.explainer: The clans of Asher are listed.

verses.text: Of the sons of Beriah: of Heber, the family of the Heberites; of Malchiel, the family of the Malchielites.
verses.explainer: Through Beriah: the Heberites and Malkielites.

verses.text: The name of the daughter of Asher was Serah.
verses.explainer: Asher's daughter Serah is mentioned -- one of very few women named in census lists.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Serah daughter of Asher appears in both census lists (Genesis 46:17 and here). Jewish tradition developed extensive legends about her, including that she lived for centuries and was the one who identified Moses as the true redeemer.

verses.text: These are the families of the sons of Asher according to those who were counted of them, fifty-three thousand and four hundred.
verses.explainer: These are the clans of Asher: 53,400.

verses.text: The sons of Naphtali after their families: of Jahzeel, the family of the Jahzeelites; of Guni, the family of the Gunites;
verses.explainer: The clans of Naphtali are listed.

verses.text: of Jezer, the family of the Jezerites; of Shillem, the family of the Shillemites.
verses.explainer: The Jezerites and Shillemites.

verses.text: These are the families of Naphtali according to their families; and those who were counted of them were forty-five thousand four hundred.
verses.explainer: These are the clans of Naphtali: 45,400.

verses.text: These are those who were counted of the children of Israel, six hundred one thousand seven hundred thirty.
verses.explainer: The total number of the men of Israel: 601,730.

thenVsNow.then: The total dropped by only 1,820 from the first census (603,550). Despite an entire generation dying in the wilderness, the nation's military strength was virtually unchanged. God preserved the people even while judging the generation.
thenVsNow.now: God's purposes survive generational transitions. The work of God is never limited to a single generation.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord tells Moses that the land is to be distributed as an inheritance based on the number of names in each tribe.

verses.text: “To these the land shall be divided for an inheritance according to the number of names.
verses.explainer: The land is to be divided among the tribes as an inheritance.

verses.text: To the more you shall give the more inheritance, and to the fewer you shall give the less inheritance. To everyone according to those who were counted of him shall his inheritance be given.
verses.explainer: Larger tribes receive larger allotments, smaller tribes receive smaller ones -- proportional distribution.

verses.text: Notwithstanding, the land shall be divided by lot. According to the names of the tribes of their fathers they shall inherit.
verses.explainer: The land is distributed by lot -- divine guidance determines which area goes to which tribe, not human preference.

verses.text: According to the lot shall their inheritance be divided between the more and the fewer.”
verses.explainer: Each inheritance is distributed according to the lot, whether for large or small groups.

verses.text: These are those who were counted of the Levites after their families: of Gershon, the family of the Gershonites; of Kohath, the family of the Kohathites; of Merari, the family of the Merarites.
verses.explainer: The Levites counted by their clans: Gershonites, Kohathites, and Merarites.

verses.text: These are the families of Levi: the family of the Libnites, the family of the Hebronites, the family of the Mahlites, the family of the Mushites, the family of the Korahites. Kohath became the father of Amram.
verses.explainer: Levite clans include the Libnites, Hebronites, Mahlites, Mushites, and Korahites. Kohath was the father of Amram.

verses.text: The name of Amram’s wife was Jochebed, the daughter of Levi, who was born to Levi in Egypt. She bore to Amram Aaron and Moses, and Miriam their sister.
verses.explainer: Amram's wife was Jochebed, a daughter of Levi born in Egypt. She bore Amram's children: Aaron, Moses, and their sister Miriam.

verses.text: To Aaron were born Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar.
verses.explainer: The sons of Aaron: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.

verses.text: Nadab and Abihu died when they offered strange fire before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Nadab and Abihu died when they offered unauthorized fire before the Lord.

crossReferences.reference: Leviticus 10:1-2 -- The death of Nadab and Abihu for offering 'strange fire' is one of the most sobering events in the Pentateuch.
crossReferences.connection: Leviticus 10:1-2 -- The death of Nadab and Abihu for offering 'strange fire' is one of the most sobering events in the Pentateuch.

verses.text: Those who were counted of them were twenty-three thousand, every male from a month old and upward; for they were not counted among the children of Israel, because there was no inheritance given them among the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: All the Levite males one month old or more numbered 23,000. They were not counted with the other Israelites because they received no land inheritance.

verses.text: These are those who were counted by Moses and Eleazar the priest, who counted the children of Israel in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho.
verses.explainer: These are the ones counted by Moses and Eleazar on the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho.

verses.text: But among these there was not a man of them who were counted by Moses and Aaron the priest, who counted the children of Israel in the wilderness of Sinai.
verses.explainer: Not one of them was among those counted by Moses and Aaron in the first census in the Desert of Sinai.

verses.text: For Yahweh had said of them, “They shall surely die in the wilderness.” There was not a man left of them, except Caleb the son of Jephunneh, and Joshua the son of Nun.
verses.explainer: The Lord had told those Israelites they would surely die in the wilderness. Not one of them was left except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.

crossReferences.reference: Numbers 14:29-30 -- God's declaration that only Joshua and Caleb would survive to enter the land is confirmed here as fulfilled.
crossReferences.connection: Numbers 14:29-30 -- God's declaration that only Joshua and Caleb would survive to enter the land is confirmed here as fulfilled.

crossReferences.reference: Hebrews 3:16-19 -- The author of Hebrews uses this generation as a warning: 'Who were they who heard and rebelled? Were they not all those Moses led out of Egypt?'
crossReferences.connection: Hebrews 3:16-19 -- The author of Hebrews uses this generation as a warning: 'Who were they who heard and rebelled? Were they not all those Moses led out of Egypt?'

explainer.summary: Two major events: First, the five daughters of Zelophehad petition Moses for the right to inherit their father's land since he died without sons. God rules in their favor, establishing a landmark legal precedent for female inheritance. Second, God tells Moses to ascend Mount Abarim to see the Promised Land he will never enter, then commissions Joshua as his successor through a public ceremony of authority transfer.
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter handles two critical transitions: the expansion of inheritance law to include daughters, and the formal commissioning of Joshua as Moses' successor. Both look forward to the land settlement, addressing questions of property (who inherits?) and leadership (who leads?).

wordStudies.significance: Moses lays his hands on Joshua in a public commissioning ceremony. 'Semikhah' (the laying on of hands) became the standard ordination ritual in Judaism, symbolizing the transfer of authority and blessing from one generation to the next.

verses.text: Then the daughters of Zelophehad, the son of Hepher, the son of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh, of the families of Manasseh the son of Joseph came near. These are the names of his daughters: Mahlah, Noah, and Hoglah, and Milcah, and Tirzah.
verses.explainer: The daughters of Zelophehad -- Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah -- come forward. They belong to the clan of Manasseh son of Joseph.

verses.text: They stood before Moses, and before Eleazar the priest, and before the princes and all the congregation, at the door of the Tent of Meeting, saying,
verses.explainer: They stand before Moses, Eleazar the priest, the leaders, and the whole assembly at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting -- a public petition.

verses.text: “Our father died in the wilderness. He was not among the company of those who gathered themselves together against Yahweh in the company of Korah, but he died in his own sin. He had no sons.
verses.explainer: Their argument: 'Our father died in the wilderness. He was not among Korah's followers -- he died for his own sin. He had no sons.'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The daughters carefully distinguish their father from Korah's rebels to prevent the argument that his name should be blotted out as punishment. He died the normal death of the wilderness generation, not as a result of specific rebellion.

verses.text: Why should the name of our father be taken away from among his family, because he had no son? Give to us a possession among the brothers of our father.”
verses.explainer: Their question: 'Why should our father's name disappear from his clan because he had no son? Give us property among our father's relatives.'

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient Near East, inheritance passed exclusively through sons. A man without sons effectively disappeared from the tribal register. The daughters' petition challenged this fundamental assumption.
thenVsNow.now: The daughters' courage in petitioning for justice within a patriarchal system has inspired movements for women's legal rights throughout history. They worked within the system to expand it.

verses.text: Moses brought their cause before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Moses brings their case before the Lord -- he does not dismiss it or rule on his own authority.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord's response: the daughters of Zelophehad are right.

verses.text: “The daughters of Zelophehad speak right. You shall surely give them a possession of an inheritance among their father’s brothers. You shall cause the inheritance of their father to pass to them.
verses.explainer: God commands: give them property as an inheritance among their father's relatives. Transfer their father's inheritance to them.

verses.text: You shall speak to the children of Israel, saying, ‘If a man dies, and has no son, then you shall cause his inheritance to pass to his daughter.
verses.explainer: A new law is established: if a man dies without a son, his inheritance passes to his daughter.

verses.text: If he has no daughter, then you shall give his inheritance to his brothers.
verses.explainer: If he has no daughter, the inheritance passes to his brothers.

verses.text: If he has no brothers, then you shall give his inheritance to his father’s brothers.
verses.explainer: If he has no brothers, it passes to his father's brothers.

verses.text: If his father has no brothers, then you shall give his inheritance to his kinsman who is next to him of his family, and he shall possess it. This shall be a statute and ordinance for the children of Israel, as Yahweh commanded Moses.’”
verses.explainer: If his father has no brothers, it passes to the nearest relative in his clan. This becomes a legal statute for Israel.

crossReferences.reference: Numbers 36:1-12 -- The ruling is later modified to require that inheriting daughters marry within their own tribe, to prevent land from transferring between tribes.
crossReferences.connection: Numbers 36:1-12 -- The ruling is later modified to require that inheriting daughters marry within their own tribe, to prevent land from transferring between tribes.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Go up into this mountain of Abarim, and see the land which I have given to the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: God tells Moses to go up Mount Abarim and view the Promised Land. He will see it but not enter it.

verses.text: When you have seen it, you also shall be gathered to your people, as Aaron your brother was gathered;
verses.explainer: After viewing the land, Moses will be gathered to his people, just as Aaron was gathered.

verses.text: because in the strife of the congregation, you rebelled against my word in the wilderness of Zin, to honor me as holy at the waters before their eyes.” (These are the waters of Meribah of Kadesh in the wilderness of Zin.)
verses.explainer: The reason: Moses and Aaron rebelled against God's command at the waters of Meribah in Kadesh by failing to honor God as holy before the people.

crossReferences.reference: Numbers 20:12 -- The original verdict at Meribah is repeated here, making clear that God has not changed his decision despite Moses' faithfulness in all other matters.
crossReferences.connection: Numbers 20:12 -- The original verdict at Meribah is repeated here, making clear that God has not changed his decision despite Moses' faithfulness in all other matters.

verses.text: Moses spoke to Yahweh, saying,
verses.explainer: Moses does not argue or plead (he does that in Deuteronomy 3:23-26). Instead, he immediately asks God to appoint a successor.

thenVsNow.then: Moses' selfless response -- prioritizing the community's need for leadership over his own grief -- reveals the depth of his character. He asks not for mercy but for continuity.
thenVsNow.now: Mature leadership thinks about succession. The willingness to plan for one's replacement is a sign of genuine concern for the mission rather than personal position.

verses.text: “Let Yahweh, the God of the spirits of all flesh, appoint a man over the congregation,
verses.explainer: Moses asks the Lord, 'the God who gives breath to all living things,' to appoint someone over the community.

verses.text: who may go out before them, and who may come in before them, and who may lead them out, and who may bring them in; that the congregation of Yahweh may not be as sheep which have no shepherd.”
verses.explainer: The leader should go out and come in before them, leading them out and bringing them in, so that the Lord's people will not be like sheep without a shepherd.

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 9:36 -- Jesus uses this same image when he sees the crowds: 'he had compassion on them, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd.'
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 9:36 -- Jesus uses this same image when he sees the crowds: 'he had compassion on them, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd.'

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Take Joshua the son of Nun, a man in whom is the Spirit, and lay your hand on him.
verses.explainer: God selects Joshua son of Nun, 'a man in whom is the spirit,' and tells Moses to lay his hand on him.

verses.text: Set him before Eleazar the priest, and before all the congregation; and commission him in their sight.
verses.explainer: Moses is to stand Joshua before Eleazar the priest and the whole assembly and commission him publicly.

verses.text: You shall give authority to him, that all the congregation of the children of Israel may obey.
verses.explainer: Moses is to give Joshua some of his authority so that the whole community will obey him.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Hebrew says Moses gave Joshua some of his 'hod' (splendor/majesty). Joshua receives a portion but not all of Moses' authority. Moses was unique; Joshua would lead differently -- through Eleazar and the Urim rather than through direct divine revelation.

verses.text: He shall stand before Eleazar the priest, who shall inquire for him by the judgment of the Urim before Yahweh. At his word they shall go out, and at his word they shall come in, both he, and all the children of Israel with him, even all the congregation.”
verses.explainer: Joshua will stand before Eleazar, who will use the Urim to inquire of the Lord for him. At the priest's word, Joshua and all Israel will go out and come in.

verses.text: Moses did as Yahweh commanded him. He took Joshua, and set him before Eleazar the priest, and before all the congregation.
verses.explainer: Moses does as the Lord commands: he takes Joshua and has him stand before Eleazar and the whole assembly.

verses.text: He laid his hands on him, and commissioned him, as Yahweh spoke by Moses.
verses.explainer: Moses lays his hands on Joshua and commissions him, just as the Lord instructed.

crossReferences.reference: Deuteronomy 34:9 -- 'Now Joshua son of Nun was filled with the spirit of wisdom because Moses had laid his hands on him.' The commissioning is effective -- authority is genuinely transferred.
crossReferences.connection: Deuteronomy 34:9 -- 'Now Joshua son of Nun was filled with the spirit of wisdom because Moses had laid his hands on him.' The commissioning is effective -- authority is genuinely transferred.

explainer.summary: God instructs Moses to remind Israel about the regular offerings: the daily burnt offering (morning and evening), the Sabbath offering (double the daily), the monthly new moon offering, and the Passover and Feast of Unleavened Bread offerings. These are 'my food offerings, my pleasing aroma' -- God's prescribed rhythm of worship that structures Israel's calendar around sacrifice.
themes: The Passover and Unleavened Bread festivals
explainer.contextInBook: Chapters 28-29 provide the sacrificial calendar for the new generation entering Canaan. While Leviticus 23 listed the festivals, these chapters specify the exact offerings for each occasion. The second generation needs clear instructions because their parents failed to teach them faithfully.

wordStudies.significance: The 'tamid' offering was the twice-daily burnt offering that formed the heartbeat of Israel's worship. It continued unbroken from the wilderness through the temple period. The cessation of the tamid offering during the Babylonian siege was considered a national catastrophe.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses with instructions about offerings to present at the appointed times.

verses.text: “Command the children of Israel, and tell them, ‘See that you present my offering, my food for my offerings made by fire, of a pleasant aroma to me, in their due season.’
verses.explainer: God commands: 'See that you present my food offering, my pleasing aroma, at the appointed time.'

verses.text: You shall tell them, ‘This is the offering made by fire which you shall offer to Yahweh: male lambs a year old without defect, two day by day, for a continual burnt offering.
verses.explainer: The daily burnt offering: two year-old lambs without defect, one in the morning and one at twilight.

crossReferences.reference: Exodus 29:38-42 -- The daily offering was first prescribed at Sinai. Its repetition here for the new generation underscores the importance of continuity.
crossReferences.connection: Exodus 29:38-42 -- The daily offering was first prescribed at Sinai. Its repetition here for the new generation underscores the importance of continuity.

verses.text: You shall offer the one lamb in the morning, and you shall offer the other lamb at evening;
verses.explainer: One lamb in the morning, the other at twilight -- framing the entire day with sacrifice.

verses.text: with one tenth of an ephah of fine flour for a meal offering, mixed with the fourth part of a hin of beaten oil.
verses.explainer: The grain offering: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with a quarter hin of pressed olive oil.

verses.text: It is a continual burnt offering, which was ordained in Mount Sinai for a pleasant aroma, an offering made by fire to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: This is the regular burnt offering instituted at Mount Sinai as a pleasing aroma, a food offering to the Lord.

verses.text: Its drink offering shall be the fourth part of a hin for the one lamb. You shall pour out a drink offering of strong drink to Yahweh in the holy place.
verses.explainer: The drink offering: a quarter hin of fermented drink for each lamb, poured out in the sanctuary area.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The drink offering was poured out, not consumed. This 'waste' of valuable wine symbolized total dedication to God -- giving something of value without any human benefit.

verses.text: The other lamb you shall offer at evening. As the meal offering of the morning, and as its drink offering, you shall offer it, an offering made by fire, for a pleasant aroma to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The second lamb is offered at twilight with the same grain and drink offerings -- a pleasing aroma to the Lord.

verses.text: “‘On the Sabbath day, you shall offer two male lambs a year old without defect, and two tenths of an ephah of fine flour for a meal offering, mixed with oil, and its drink offering:
verses.explainer: The Sabbath offering: two year-old lambs without defect, with their grain and drink offerings, in addition to the daily offering.

thenVsNow.then: The Sabbath doubled the daily offering -- rest from work meant increased worship, not decreased. The day of rest was the day of most sacrifice.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that rest should increase rather than decrease worship challenges the modern tendency to treat days off as purely personal leisure.

verses.text: this is the burnt offering of every Sabbath, besides the continual burnt offering, and its drink offering.
verses.explainer: This is the Sabbath burnt offering each week, in addition to the regular daily burnt offering and its drink offering.

verses.text: “‘In the beginnings of your months, you shall offer a burnt offering to Yahweh: two young bulls, and one ram, seven male lambs a year old without defect;
verses.explainer: The first day of each month: two young bulls, one ram, and seven male year-old lambs without defect as a burnt offering.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The new moon (Rosh Chodesh) marked the beginning of each lunar month. The increased offering acknowledged God's sovereignty over time itself.

verses.text: and three tenths of an ephah of fine flour for a meal offering, mixed with oil, for each bull; and two tenth parts of fine flour for a meal offering, mixed with oil, for the one ram;
verses.explainer: The grain offering for each bull: three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil.

verses.text: and one tenth part of fine flour mixed with oil for a meal offering to every lamb; for a burnt offering of a pleasant aroma, an offering made by fire to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The grain offering for the ram: two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil.

verses.text: Their drink offerings shall be half a hin of wine for a bull, and the third part of a hin for the ram, and the fourth part of a hin for a lamb. This is the burnt offering of every month throughout the months of the year.
verses.explainer: The drink offerings: half a hin of wine for a bull, a third for a ram, a quarter for a lamb. This is the monthly burnt offering for each new moon.

verses.text: One male goat for a sin offering to Yahweh; it shall be offered besides the continual burnt offering, and its drink offering.
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering to the Lord, in addition to the regular daily offering and its drink offering.

verses.text: “‘In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month, is Yahweh’s Passover.
verses.explainer: On the fourteenth day of the first month: the Lord's Passover.

crossReferences.reference: Exodus 12:1-14 -- The Passover commemorates the night when God's judgment 'passed over' Israelite homes marked with lamb's blood in Egypt.
crossReferences.connection: Exodus 12:1-14 -- The Passover commemorates the night when God's judgment 'passed over' Israelite homes marked with lamb's blood in Egypt.

verses.text: On the fifteenth day of this month shall be a feast. Unleavened bread shall be eaten for seven days.
verses.explainer: On the fifteenth day: a festival. For seven days eat bread made without yeast.

verses.text: In the first day shall be a holy convocation. You shall do no regular work;
verses.explainer: On the first day: a sacred assembly. Do no regular work.

verses.text: but you shall offer an offering made by fire, a burnt offering to Yahweh: two young bulls, and one ram, and seven male lambs a year old; they shall be to you without defect;
verses.explainer: Present a food offering of two young bulls, one ram, and seven male year-old lambs, all without defect.

verses.text: and their meal offering, fine flour mixed with oil. You shall offer three tenths for a bull, and two tenths for the ram.
verses.explainer: Their grain offerings of fine flour mixed with oil: three-tenths for each bull, two-tenths for the ram.

verses.text: You shall offer one tenth for every lamb of the seven lambs;
verses.explainer: One-tenth for each of the seven lambs.

verses.text: and one male goat for a sin offering, to make atonement for you.
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering to make atonement.

verses.text: You shall offer these besides the burnt offering of the morning, which is for a continual burnt offering.
verses.explainer: These are in addition to the regular morning burnt offering.

verses.text: In this way you shall offer daily, for seven days, the food of the offering made by fire, of a pleasant aroma to Yahweh. It shall be offered in addition to the continual burnt offering, and its drink offering.
verses.explainer: Offer these same sacrifices each day for seven days as a food offering, a pleasing aroma to the Lord, in addition to the daily offering.

verses.text: On the seventh day you shall have a holy convocation. You shall do no regular work.
verses.explainer: On the seventh day hold a sacred assembly and do no regular work.

verses.text: “‘Also in the day of the first fruits, when you offer a new meal offering to Yahweh in your feast of weeks, you shall have a holy convocation. You shall do no regular work;
verses.explainer: On the day of firstfruits (Feast of Weeks/Shavuot), when you present an offering of new grain, hold a sacred assembly and do no regular work.

thenVsNow.then: The Feast of Weeks celebrated the wheat harvest and occurred fifty days after Passover. It acknowledged that agricultural abundance came from God.
thenVsNow.now: Christians identify Pentecost (from the Greek word for 'fiftieth') with the outpouring of the Holy Spirit in Acts 2, which occurred on the Feast of Weeks.

verses.text: but you shall offer a burnt offering for a pleasant aroma to Yahweh: two young bulls, one ram, seven male lambs a year old;
verses.explainer: Present a burnt offering of two young bulls, one ram, and seven male year-old lambs as a pleasing aroma.

verses.text: and their meal offering, fine flour mixed with oil, three tenths for each bull, two tenths for the one ram,
verses.explainer: Their grain offerings: three-tenths for each bull, two-tenths for the ram.

verses.text: one tenth for every lamb of the seven lambs;
verses.explainer: One-tenth for each of the seven lambs.

verses.text: one male goat, to make atonement for you.
verses.explainer: One male goat to make atonement.

verses.text: Besides the continual burnt offering, and its meal offering, you shall offer them and their drink offerings. See that they are without defect.
verses.explainer: Prepare these in addition to the regular daily burnt offering and its grain offering. The animals must be without defect, and include their drink offerings.

explainer.summary: This chapter prescribes the offerings for the three great fall festivals: the Feast of Trumpets (Rosh Hashanah), the Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur), and the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot). The Feast of Tabernacles requires the most elaborate sacrificial program in the entire calendar -- seventy bulls over seven days, descending from thirteen on the first day to seven on the seventh, with a solemn assembly on the eighth day.
themes: Feast of Trumpets as sacred alarm
Day of Atonement as day of affliction
Feast of Tabernacles as greatest celebration
The seventy bulls and the nations
explainer.contextInBook: Together with chapter 28, this chapter gives the new generation their complete worship calendar. The fall festivals -- trumpets, atonement, tabernacles -- represent the culmination of the sacred year, moving from alarm to repentance to celebration.

wordStudies.significance: The first day of the seventh month is 'Yom Teruah' -- a day of trumpet blasting. The teruah was a series of short blasts used as an alarm signal. Jewish tradition developed this into Rosh Hashanah, the New Year, understood as a day of divine judgment.

verses.text: “‘In the seventh month, on the first day of the month, you shall have a holy convocation; you shall do no regular work: it is a day of blowing of trumpets to you.
verses.explainer: On the first day of the seventh month: a sacred assembly. Do no regular work. It is a day for you to sound the trumpets.

verses.text: You shall offer a burnt offering for a pleasant aroma to Yahweh: one young bull, one ram, seven male lambs a year old without defect;
verses.explainer: Present a burnt offering of one young bull, one ram, and seven male year-old lambs, all without defect.

verses.text: and their meal offering, fine flour mixed with oil, three tenths for the bull, two tenths for the ram,
verses.explainer: Their grain offerings: three-tenths for the bull, two-tenths for the ram.

verses.text: and one tenth for every lamb of the seven lambs;
verses.explainer: One-tenth for each of the seven lambs.

verses.text: and one male goat for a sin offering, to make atonement for you;
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering to make atonement.

verses.text: besides the burnt offering of the new moon, and its meal offering, and the continual burnt offering and its meal offering, and their drink offerings, according to their ordinance, for a pleasant aroma, an offering made by fire to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: These are in addition to the monthly and daily burnt offerings with their grain and drink offerings, as prescribed -- a pleasing aroma to the Lord.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The cumulative effect is significant: on this day, the daily offerings, the monthly new moon offerings, and the special Feast of Trumpets offerings were all presented. The total sacrifice was enormous.

verses.text: “‘On the tenth day of this seventh month you shall have a holy convocation. You shall afflict your souls. You shall do no kind of work;
verses.explainer: On the tenth day of the seventh month: a sacred assembly. You must deny yourselves and do no work.

crossReferences.reference: Leviticus 16 -- The Day of Atonement ritual is described in detail in Leviticus 16, including the scapegoat ceremony. Numbers 29 adds the specific offering quantities.
crossReferences.connection: Leviticus 16 -- The Day of Atonement ritual is described in detail in Leviticus 16, including the scapegoat ceremony. Numbers 29 adds the specific offering quantities.

verses.text: but you shall offer a burnt offering to Yahweh for a pleasant aroma: one young bull, one ram, seven male lambs a year old; all without defect;
verses.explainer: Present a burnt offering of one young bull, one ram, and seven male year-old lambs, all without defect.

verses.text: and their meal offering, fine flour mixed with oil, three tenths for the bull, two tenths for the one ram,
verses.explainer: Their grain offerings: three-tenths for the bull, two-tenths for the ram.

verses.text: one tenth for every lamb of the seven lambs:
verses.explainer: One-tenth for each of the seven lambs.

verses.text: one male goat for a sin offering; besides the sin offering of atonement, and the continual burnt offering, and its meal offering, and their drink offerings.
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the sin offering for atonement, the regular daily offering, and their grain and drink offerings.

thenVsNow.then: The Day of Atonement was the only mandated fast day in the Mosaic law. 'Denying yourselves' included fasting, abstaining from work, and deep self-examination.
thenVsNow.now: Yom Kippur remains the most solemn day in the Jewish calendar. Christians see its fulfillment in Christ's once-for-all sacrifice (Hebrews 9:11-14).

verses.text: “‘On the fifteenth day of the seventh month you shall have a holy convocation. You shall do no regular work. You shall keep a feast to Yahweh seven days.
verses.explainer: On the fifteenth day of the seventh month: a sacred assembly. Do no regular work. Celebrate a festival to the Lord for seven days.

verses.text: You shall offer a burnt offering, an offering made by fire, of a pleasant aroma to Yahweh: thirteen young bulls, two rams, fourteen male lambs a year old; all without defect;
verses.explainer: Present a burnt offering of thirteen young bulls, two rams, and fourteen male year-old lambs, all without defect.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The thirteen bulls on day one begin a descending pattern: 13, 12, 11, 10, 9, 8, 7 -- totaling seventy bulls over seven days. Rabbinic tradition connected the seventy bulls to the seventy nations of Genesis 10, interpreting Israel as offering sacrifices on behalf of all humanity.

verses.text: and their meal offering, fine flour mixed with oil, three tenths for every bull of the thirteen bulls, two tenths for each ram of the two rams,
verses.explainer: Their grain offerings: three-tenths for each of the thirteen bulls, two-tenths for each of the two rams.

verses.text: and one tenth for every lamb of the fourteen lambs;
verses.explainer: One-tenth for each of the fourteen lambs.

verses.text: and one male goat for a sin offering, besides the continual burnt offering, its meal offering, and its drink offering.
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular daily burnt offering.

verses.text: “‘On the second day you shall offer twelve young bulls, two rams, fourteen male lambs a year old without defect;
verses.explainer: On the second day: twelve young bulls, two rams, fourteen male year-old lambs without defect.

verses.text: and their meal offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, after the ordinance;
verses.explainer: With their grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.

verses.text: and one male goat for a sin offering; besides the continual burnt offering, with its meal offering and their drink offerings.
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular daily offering.

verses.text: “‘On the third day eleven bulls, two rams, fourteen male lambs a year old without defect;
verses.explainer: On the third day: eleven bulls, two rams, fourteen lambs without defect.

verses.text: and their meal offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, after the ordinance;
verses.explainer: With their grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.

verses.text: and one male goat for a sin offering; besides the continual burnt offering, and its meal offering, and its drink offering.
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular daily offering.

verses.text: “‘On the fourth day ten bulls, two rams, fourteen male lambs a year old without defect;
verses.explainer: On the fourth day: ten bulls, two rams, fourteen lambs without defect.

verses.text: their meal offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, after the ordinance;
verses.explainer: With their grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.

verses.text: and one male goat for a sin offering; besides the continual burnt offering, its meal offering, and its drink offering.
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular daily offering.

verses.text: “‘On the fifth day nine bulls, two rams, fourteen male lambs a year old without defect;
verses.explainer: On the fifth day: nine bulls, two rams, fourteen lambs without defect.

verses.text: and their meal offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, after the ordinance;
verses.explainer: With their grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.

verses.text: and one male goat for a sin offering, besides the continual burnt offering, and its meal offering, and its drink offering.
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular daily offering.

verses.text: “‘On the sixth day eight bulls, two rams, fourteen male lambs a year old without defect;
verses.explainer: On the sixth day: eight bulls, two rams, fourteen lambs without defect.

verses.text: and their meal offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, after the ordinance;
verses.explainer: With their grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.

verses.text: and one male goat for a sin offering; besides the continual burnt offering, its meal offering, and the drink offerings of it.
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular daily offering.

verses.text: “‘On the seventh day seven bulls, two rams, fourteen male lambs a year old without defect;
verses.explainer: On the seventh day: seven bulls, two rams, fourteen lambs without defect.

verses.text: and their meal offering and their drink offerings for the bulls, for the rams, and for the lambs, according to their number, after the ordinance;
verses.explainer: With their grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.

verses.text: and one male goat for a sin offering; besides the continual burnt offering, its meal offering, and its drink offering.
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular daily offering.

verses.text: “‘On the eighth day you shall have a solemn assembly: you shall do no regular work;
verses.explainer: On the eighth day: hold a solemn assembly. Do no regular work.

crossReferences.reference: John 7:37 -- 'On the last and greatest day of the festival, Jesus stood and said in a loud voice, "Let anyone who is thirsty come to me and drink."'
crossReferences.connection: John 7:37 -- 'On the last and greatest day of the festival, Jesus stood and said in a loud voice, "Let anyone who is thirsty come to me and drink."'

verses.text: but you shall offer a burnt offering, an offering made by fire, of a pleasant aroma to Yahweh: one bull, one ram, seven male lambs a year old without defect;
verses.explainer: Present a burnt offering of one bull, one ram, and seven male year-old lambs, all without defect.

thenVsNow.then: The dramatic reduction to a single bull on the eighth day contrasts with the thirteen on day one. After the grand national celebration, the final day is intimate and restrained.
thenVsNow.now: The movement from abundance to simplicity mirrors the spiritual principle that the deepest encounters with God are often the quietest.

verses.text: their meal offering and their drink offerings for the bull, for the ram, and for the lambs, shall be according to their number, after the ordinance:
verses.explainer: With their grain and drink offerings for the bull, ram, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.

verses.text: and one male goat for a sin offering, besides the continual burnt offering, with its meal offering, and its drink offering.
verses.explainer: One male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular daily offering.

verses.text: “‘You shall offer these to Yahweh in your set feasts, besides your vows, and your freewill offerings, for your burnt offerings, and for your meal offerings, and for your drink offerings, and for your peace offerings.’”
verses.explainer: These are what you are to present to the Lord at your appointed festivals: your burnt offerings, grain offerings, drink offerings, and fellowship offerings, in addition to your vows and freewill offerings.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The phrase 'in addition to your vows and freewill offerings' is important: the prescribed calendar was the minimum. Individual devotion could add voluntary offerings beyond the required schedule.

verses.text: Moses told the children of Israel according to all that Yahweh commanded Moses.
verses.explainer: Moses tells the Israelites everything the Lord has commanded him.

explainer.summary: This chapter establishes the law of vows, with special attention to vows made by women. A man's vow is binding without qualification. A woman's vow can be annulled by her father (if unmarried) or her husband on the day he hears of it. If the responsible male says nothing on the day he learns of the vow, it stands. The chapter reflects the patriarchal social structure while creating legal protections for women within it.
themes: The binding nature of spoken vows
Protection of women in a patriarchal system
explainer.contextInBook: Placed between the festival calendar and the war with Midian, this chapter addresses a practical issue: vows made to God are irrevocable except under specific circumstances. As Israel prepares for conquest, the seriousness of commitments made to God is reinforced.

wordStudies.significance: A 'neder' was a voluntary commitment to God -- usually a promise to offer a sacrifice, abstain from something, or dedicate a possession if God answered a prayer. Once spoken, it was legally and spiritually binding.

verses.text: Moses spoke to the heads of the tribes of the children of Israel, saying, “This is the thing which Yahweh has commanded.
verses.explainer: Moses speaks to the heads of the tribes of Israel about the Lord's command concerning vows.

verses.text: When a man vows a vow to Yahweh, or swears an oath to bind his soul with a bond, he shall not break his word. He shall do according to all that proceeds out of his mouth.
verses.explainer: When a man makes a vow to the Lord or takes an oath to obligate himself by a pledge, he must not break his word. He must do everything he said.

crossReferences.reference: Ecclesiastes 5:4-5 -- 'When you make a vow to God, do not delay to fulfill it. He has no pleasure in fools; fulfill your vow. It is better not to make a vow than to make one and not fulfill it.'
crossReferences.connection: Ecclesiastes 5:4-5 -- 'When you make a vow to God, do not delay to fulfill it. He has no pleasure in fools; fulfill your vow. It is better not to make a vow than to make one and not fulfill it.'

thenVsNow.then: In a culture without written contracts, a man's spoken word was his bond. Breaking a vow was not just dishonest but sacrilegious -- it was lying to God.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that commitments to God must be honored remains foundational. Modern casualness about promises contrasts sharply with the biblical gravity attached to spoken commitments.

verses.text: “Also when a woman vows a vow to Yahweh, and binds herself by a bond, being in her father’s house, in her youth,
verses.explainer: When a young woman still living in her father's household makes a vow or obligates herself by a pledge --

verses.text: and her father hears her vow, and her bond with which she has bound her soul, and her father holds his peace at her; then all her vows shall stand, and every bond with which she has bound her soul shall stand.
verses.explainer: If her father hears about her vow and says nothing, all her vows and pledges stand.

verses.text: But if her father forbids her in the day that he hears, none of her vows, or of her bonds with which she has bound her soul, shall stand. Yahweh will forgive her, because her father has forbidden her.
verses.explainer: But if her father forbids her on the day he hears about it, none of her vows or pledges will stand. The Lord will release her because her father has forbidden her.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The father's authority to annul was not unlimited -- it had to be exercised on the day he heard about the vow. If he waited, his silence constituted ratification. This created a practical check on arbitrary paternal power.

verses.text: “If she has a husband, while her vows are on her, or the rash utterance of her lips, with which she has bound her soul,
verses.explainer: If she marries after making a vow or after her lips utter a rash promise --

verses.text: and her husband hears it, and hold his peace at her in the day that he hears it; then her vows shall stand, and her bonds with which she has bound her soul shall stand.
verses.explainer: And her husband hears about it but says nothing on that day, then her vows and pledges stand.

verses.text: But if her husband forbids her in the day that he hears it, then he shall make void her vow which is on her, and the rash utterance of her lips, with which she has bound her soul. Yahweh will forgive her.
verses.explainer: But if her husband forbids her when he hears about it, he nullifies the vow or the rash promise, and the Lord will release her.

verses.text: “But the vow of a widow, or of her who is divorced, everything with which she has bound her soul, shall stand against her.
verses.explainer: A vow made by a widow or divorced woman is binding on her -- she has no male authority figure who can annul it.

thenVsNow.then: The independent legal standing of widows and divorced women in this law is notable. Without a male guardian, their vows were treated as a man's -- fully binding.
thenVsNow.now: This verse implicitly recognizes women's full legal capacity when not under patriarchal authority, suggesting the guardianship provisions were about social structure rather than inherent incapacity.

verses.text: “If she vowed in her husband’s house, or bound her soul by a bond with an oath,
verses.explainer: If a woman living with her husband makes a vow or obligates herself by an oath --

verses.text: and her husband heard it, and held his peace at her, and didn’t disallow her; then all her vows shall stand, and every bond with which she bound her soul shall stand.
verses.explainer: And her husband hears about it but says nothing and does not forbid her, then all her vows and pledges stand.

verses.text: But if her husband made them null and void in the day that he heard them, then whatever proceeded out of her lips concerning her vows, or concerning the bond of her soul, shall not stand. Her husband has made them void. Yahweh will forgive her.
verses.explainer: But if her husband nullifies them on the day he hears about them, nothing she said -- vows or pledges -- is binding. The Lord will release her.

verses.text: Every vow, and every binding oath to afflict the soul, her husband may establish it, or her husband may make it void.
verses.explainer: Her husband may confirm or nullify any vow or any sworn pledge to deny herself.

verses.text: But if her husband altogether hold his peace at her from day to day, then he establishes all her vows, or all her bonds, which are on her. He has established them, because he held his peace at her in the day that he heard them.
verses.explainer: If her husband says nothing to her day after day, he confirms all her vows and pledges. He confirms them by saying nothing on the day he hears about them.

verses.text: But if he shall make them null and void after that he has heard them, then he shall bear her iniquity.”
verses.explainer: If he annuls them after the day he hears about them, then he will be responsible for her wrong -- she is released but his guilt replaces hers.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: This is a remarkable provision: if the husband waits too long to object and then annuls, the guilt for any unfulfilled vow falls on him, not on the woman. The law penalizes male indecisiveness and protects the woman from blame.

verses.text: These are the statutes which Yahweh commanded Moses, between a man and his wife, between a father and his daughter, being in her youth, in her father’s house.
verses.explainer: These are the regulations the Lord gave Moses concerning relationships between a man and his wife, and between a father and his young daughter still living at home.

explainer.summary: God commands Moses to take vengeance on the Midianites for their role in the Baal Peor apostasy before he dies. Twelve thousand Israelite soldiers (1,000 per tribe) defeat Midian, killing all the men including the five kings of Midian and Balaam son of Beor. The plunder is divided between the warriors and the community, with portions set aside for the Levites and the Lord. The officers report that not a single Israelite soldier was killed -- an extraordinary testimony to divine protection.
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter fulfills God's command in 25:16-18 to attack Midian for the Baal Peor incident. It is Moses' final military act before his death. The discovery that Balaam was with Midian (v. 8) confirms that he counseled the seduction strategy.

wordStudies.significance: The war is described as God's vengeance (naqam), not Israel's. The concept is judicial rather than emotional -- it is divine retribution for a specific offense (the Baal Peor seduction). Israel acts as God's instrument of justice.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord tells Moses to take vengeance on the Midianites for the Israelites. After that, Moses will be gathered to his people.

verses.text: “Avenge the children of Israel for the Midianites. Afterward you shall be gathered to your people.”
verses.explainer: Moses must carry out the Lord's vengeance on Midian, and then he will die.

verses.text: Moses spoke to the people, saying, “Arm men from among you for the war, that they may go against Midian, to execute Yahweh’s vengeance on Midian.
verses.explainer: Moses tells the people to arm men for war against Midian to carry out the Lord's vengeance.

verses.text: Of every tribe one thousand, throughout all the tribes of Israel, you shall send to the war.”
verses.explainer: Send one thousand from each tribe -- twelve thousand armed for battle.

verses.text: So there were delivered, out of the thousands of Israel, a thousand of every tribe, twelve thousand armed for war.
verses.explainer: Twelve thousand men are supplied, one thousand from each tribe.

verses.text: Moses sent them, one thousand of every tribe, to the war, them and Phinehas the son of Eleazar the priest, to the war, with the vessels of the sanctuary and the trumpets for the alarm in his hand.
verses.explainer: Moses sends them to battle with Phinehas son of Eleazar, who carries the sacred articles and the trumpets for signaling.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Phinehas leads rather than a military commander, reflecting the nature of this campaign as holy war. His zeal at Peor (chapter 25) qualifies him to lead the retaliatory campaign.

verses.text: They fought against Midian, as Yahweh commanded Moses. They killed every male.
verses.explainer: They fight against Midian as the Lord commanded Moses, and kill every man.

verses.text: They killed the kings of Midian with the rest of their slain: Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, and Reba, the five kings of Midian. They also killed Balaam the son of Beor with the sword.
verses.explainer: Among those killed are the five kings of Midian: Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, and Reba. They also kill Balaam son of Beor with the sword.

crossReferences.reference: 2 Peter 2:15-16 -- Peter cites Balaam as one who 'loved the wages of wickedness' -- his prophecies were genuine but his character was corrupt.
crossReferences.connection: 2 Peter 2:15-16 -- Peter cites Balaam as one who 'loved the wages of wickedness' -- his prophecies were genuine but his character was corrupt.

verses.text: The children of Israel took the women of Midian captive with their little ones; and all their livestock, all their flocks, and all their goods, they took as plunder.
verses.explainer: Israel captures the Midianite women, children, herds, flocks, and all their goods.

verses.text: All their cities in the places in which they lived, and all their encampments, they burned with fire.
verses.explainer: They burn all the Midianite towns and camps.

verses.text: They took all the captives, and all the plunder, both of man and of animal.
verses.explainer: They take all the plunder and spoils, including people and animals.

verses.text: They brought the captives, and the prey, and the plunder, to Moses, and to Eleazar the priest, and to the congregation of the children of Israel, to the camp at the plains of Moab, which are by the Jordan at Jericho.
verses.explainer: They bring the captives, spoils, and plunder to Moses, Eleazar, and the whole assembly at the camp on the plains of Moab.

verses.text: Moses, and Eleazar the priest, and all the princes of the congregation, went out to meet them outside of the camp.
verses.explainer: Moses, Eleazar, and all the community leaders go out to meet the returning army outside the camp.

verses.text: Moses was angry with the officers of the army, the captains of thousands and the captains of hundreds, who came from the service of the war.
verses.explainer: Moses is angry with the military officers for allowing the women to live.

thenVsNow.then: Moses' anger stems from the fact that these specific women were the agents of the Baal Peor seduction. Sparing them risked repeating the very sin the war was meant to punish.
thenVsNow.now: This passage raises difficult moral questions about ancient warfare that cannot be resolved by modern ethical standards alone. The text operates within a framework of holy war that the later biblical tradition itself moves beyond.

verses.text: Moses said to them, “Have you saved all the women alive?
verses.explainer: Moses asks: 'Have you allowed all the women to live?'

verses.text: Behold, these caused the children of Israel, through the counsel of Balaam, to commit trespass against Yahweh in the matter of Peor, and so the plague was among the congregation of Yahweh.
verses.explainer: He reminds them: 'These are the very women who followed Balaam's advice and turned the Israelites away from the Lord in the Peor incident, so that a plague struck the Lord's people.'

crossReferences.reference: Revelation 2:14 -- Jesus references 'the teaching of Balaam, who taught Balak to entice the Israelites to sin by eating food sacrificed to idols and by committing sexual immorality.'
crossReferences.connection: Revelation 2:14 -- Jesus references 'the teaching of Balaam, who taught Balak to entice the Israelites to sin by eating food sacrificed to idols and by committing sexual immorality.'

verses.text: Now therefore kill every male among the little ones, and kill every woman who has known man by lying with him.
verses.explainer: Moses commands that the boys and non-virgin women be killed.

verses.text: But all the girls, who have not known man by lying with him, keep alive for yourselves.
verses.explainer: The young girls who have not been with a man are to be spared.

verses.text: “Encamp outside of the camp seven days. Whoever has killed any person, and whoever has touched any slain, purify yourselves on the third day and on the seventh day, you and your captives.
verses.explainer: Anyone who has killed a person or touched a dead body must stay outside the camp for seven days and purify themselves on the third and seventh days.

crossReferences.reference: Numbers 19:11-12 -- The purification ritual for corpse contamination from chapter 19 is applied here. Even justified killing in holy war creates ritual impurity.
crossReferences.connection: Numbers 19:11-12 -- The purification ritual for corpse contamination from chapter 19 is applied here. Even justified killing in holy war creates ritual impurity.

verses.text: As to every garment, and all that is made of skin, and all work of goats’ hair, and all things made of wood, you shall purify yourselves.”
verses.explainer: Purify every garment, leather article, goat-hair product, and wooden object.

verses.text: Eleazar the priest said to the men of war who went to the battle, “This is the statute of the law which Yahweh has commanded Moses:
verses.explainer: Eleazar tells the soldiers the requirement of the law that the Lord gave Moses regarding purification.

verses.text: however the gold, and the silver, the brass, the iron, the tin, and the lead,
verses.explainer: Gold, silver, bronze, iron, tin, and lead -- any metal that can withstand fire --

verses.text: everything that may withstand the fire, you shall make to go through the fire, and it shall be clean; nevertheless it shall be purified with the water for impurity. All that doesn’t withstand the fire you shall make to go through the water.
verses.explainer: Must be passed through fire to be purified, then also through the water of cleansing. Everything that cannot withstand fire must be put through water.

verses.text: You shall wash your clothes on the seventh day, and you shall be clean. Afterward you shall come into the camp.”
verses.explainer: On the seventh day, wash your clothes and you will be clean. Then you may come into the camp.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord tells Moses to count all the plunder -- people and animals.

verses.text: “Count the plunder that was taken, both of man and of animal, you, and Eleazar the priest, and the heads of the fathers’ households of the congregation;
verses.explainer: Moses and Eleazar the priest and the family heads are to count the plunder together.

verses.text: and divide the plunder into two parts: between the men skilled in war, who went out to battle, and all the congregation.
verses.explainer: Divide the plunder equally between the soldiers who fought and the rest of the community.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The fifty-fifty split between combatants and non-combatants acknowledged that the entire community contributed to the war effort -- those who stayed behind supported those who fought. David later adopted this same principle (1 Samuel 30:24-25).

verses.text: Levy a tribute to Yahweh of the men of war who went out to battle: one soul of five hundred; of the persons, of the cattle, of the donkeys, and of the flocks.
verses.explainer: From the soldiers' half, set aside a tribute for the Lord: one out of every five hundred persons, cattle, donkeys, and sheep.

verses.text: Take it from their half, and give it to Eleazar the priest, for Yahweh’s wave offering.
verses.explainer: Take this tribute from the soldiers' half and give it to Eleazar the priest as the Lord's share.

verses.text: Of the children of Israel’s half, you shall take one drawn out of every fifty, of the persons, of the cattle, of the donkeys, and of the flocks, of all the livestock, and give them to the Levites, who perform the duty of Yahweh’s tabernacle.”
verses.explainer: From the community's half, select one out of every fifty persons, cattle, donkeys, sheep, and other animals, and give them to the Levites.

verses.text: Moses and Eleazar the priest did as Yahweh commanded Moses.
verses.explainer: Moses and Eleazar do as the Lord commands.

verses.text: Now the plunder, over and above the booty which the men of war took, was six hundred seventy-five thousand sheep,
verses.explainer: The plunder remaining from what the soldiers took: 675,000 sheep.

verses.text: and seventy-two thousand head of cattle,
verses.explainer: 72,000 cattle.

verses.text: and sixty-one thousand donkeys,
verses.explainer: 61,000 donkeys.

verses.text: and thirty-two thousand persons in all, of the women who had not known man by lying with him.
verses.explainer: 32,000 women who had not been with a man.

verses.text: The half, which was the portion of those who went out to war, was in number three hundred thirty-seven thousand five hundred sheep:
verses.explainer: The half share for those who fought: 337,500 sheep.

verses.text: and Yahweh’s tribute of the sheep was six hundred seventy-five.
verses.explainer: The Lord's tribute from the sheep: 675.

verses.text: The cattle were thirty-six thousand; of which Yahweh’s tribute was seventy-two.
verses.explainer: 36,000 cattle, with the Lord's tribute: 72.

verses.text: The donkeys were thirty thousand five hundred; of which Yahweh’s tribute was sixty-one.
verses.explainer: 30,500 donkeys, with the Lord's tribute: 61.

verses.text: The persons were sixteen thousand; of whom Yahweh’s tribute was thirty-two persons.
verses.explainer: 16,000 people, with the Lord's tribute: 32 persons.

verses.text: Moses gave the tribute, which was Yahweh’s wave offering, to Eleazar the priest, as Yahweh commanded Moses.
verses.explainer: Moses gives the tribute to Eleazar the priest as the Lord's share, as commanded.

verses.text: Of the children of Israel’s half, which Moses divided off from the men who fought
verses.explainer: The community's half -- which Moses separated from the soldiers' portion --

verses.text: (now the congregation’s half was three hundred thirty-seven thousand five hundred sheep,
and thirty-six thousand head of cattle,
verses.explainer: 36,000 cattle.

verses.text: and thirty thousand five hundred donkeys,
verses.explainer: 30,500 donkeys.

verses.text: and sixteen thousand persons),
verses.explainer: 16,000 people.

verses.text: even of the children of Israel’s half, Moses took one drawn out of every fifty, both of man and of animal, and gave them to the Levites, who performed the duty of Yahweh’s tabernacle; as Yahweh commanded Moses.
verses.explainer: From the community's half, Moses selects one out of every fifty persons and animals and gives them to the Levites as the Lord commanded.

verses.text: The officers who were over the thousands of the army, the captains of thousands, and the captains of hundreds, came near to Moses.
verses.explainer: The officers who served over the units of the army report to Moses.

verses.text: They said to Moses, “Your servants have taken the sum of the men of war who are under our command, and there lacks not one man of us.
verses.explainer: They say: 'Your servants have counted the soldiers under our command, and not one is missing.'

verses.text: We have brought Yahweh’s offering, what every man has gotten, of jewels of gold, armlets, and bracelets, signet rings, earrings, and necklaces, to make atonement for our souls before Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: The officers present an offering to the Lord: gold articles -- armlets, bracelets, signet rings, earrings, and necklaces -- to make atonement.

verses.text: Moses and Eleazar the priest took their gold, even all worked jewels.
verses.explainer: Moses and Eleazar accept the gold from the officers -- all crafted articles.

thenVsNow.then: The voluntary offering of gold plunder expressed gratitude for the miraculous preservation of every soldier. It went beyond what was required.
thenVsNow.now: Gratitude that goes beyond the minimum is the mark of genuine faith. The officers did not have to give these gold items but chose to because they recognized what God had done.

verses.text: All the gold of the wave offering that they offered up to Yahweh, of the captains of thousands, and of the captains of hundreds, was sixteen thousand seven hundred fifty shekels.
verses.explainer: The total gold from the commanders: 16,750 shekels (approximately 420 pounds).

verses.text: The men of war had taken booty, every man for himself.
verses.explainer: Each soldier had taken plunder for himself -- the officers' gift was above and beyond personal spoils.

verses.text: Moses and Eleazar the priest took the gold of the captains of thousands and of hundreds, and brought it into the Tent of Meeting, for a memorial for the children of Israel before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Moses and Eleazar receive the gold and bring it into the Tent of Meeting as a memorial for the Israelites before the Lord.

explainer.summary: The tribes of Reuben and Gad, who have large herds, see that the conquered Transjordan territory is ideal for livestock.
