They ask Moses to let them settle east of the Jordan rather than crossing into Canaan. Moses erupts in anger, comparing them to the ten spies who discouraged Israel forty years earlier. After negotiation, they agree to send their warriors across the Jordan to fight alongside the other tribes before returning to their Transjordan inheritance. Half the tribe of Manasseh joins the arrangement.
themes: Moses fears a repeat of Kadesh-barnea
Responsibility to the community despite personal preference
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter raises the question of whether settling east of the Jordan constitutes a failure of faith -- choosing less than God's best. Moses treats the request as potentially catastrophic because it echoes the spy narrative. The negotiated solution requires Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh to fight for their brothers before enjoying their own inheritance.

wordStudies.significance: The word 'miqneh' (from 'qanah,' to acquire) refers to livestock as moveable wealth. The tribes' identity was defined by their herds. Their request prioritized economic interest ('this land is good for livestock') over the shared mission of conquering Canaan.

verses.text: Now the children of Reuben and the children of Gad had a very great multitude of livestock. When they saw the land of Jazer, and the land of Gilead, that behold, the place was a place for livestock;
verses.explainer: The tribes of Reuben and Gad have very large herds and flocks. They see that the lands of Jazer and Gilead are suitable for livestock.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Transjordan territory captured from Sihon and Og was well-watered grassland -- ideal for pastoralism. The economic logic of the request was sound, but Moses questioned the spiritual implications.

verses.text: the children of Gad and the children of Reuben came and spoke to Moses, and to Eleazar the priest, and to the princes of the congregation, saying,
verses.explainer: They come to Moses, Eleazar, and the leaders of the community with their request.

verses.text: “Ataroth, Dibon, Jazer, Nimrah, Heshbon, Elealeh, Sebam, Nebo, and Beon,
verses.explainer: They list the captured cities: Ataroth, Dibon, Jazer, Nimrah, Heshbon, Elealeh, Sebam, Nebo, and Beon.

verses.text: the land which Yahweh struck before the congregation of Israel, is a land for livestock; and your servants have livestock.”
verses.explainer: The land the Lord subdued before the people of Israel is suitable for livestock, and they have livestock.

verses.text: They said, “If we have found favor in your sight, let this land be given to your servants for a possession. Don’t bring us over the Jordan.”
verses.explainer: Their request: 'If we have found favor in your eyes, let this land be given to us as our possession. Do not make us cross the Jordan.'

verses.text: Moses said to the children of Gad, and to the children of Reuben, “Shall your brothers go to the war, and shall you sit here?
verses.explainer: Moses responds with anger: 'Should your fellow Israelites go to war while you sit here?'

thenVsNow.then: Moses immediately sees the parallel to the spy narrative: part of the community wanting to opt out while others face the danger of conquest.
thenVsNow.now: The tension between individual benefit and communal responsibility recurs in every community. Pursuing personal advantage at the expense of the group undermines shared mission.

verses.text: Why do you discourage the heart of the children of Israel from going over into the land which Yahweh has given them?
verses.explainer: Moses warns: 'Why do you discourage the Israelites from crossing over into the land the Lord has given them?'

verses.text: Your fathers did so when I sent them from Kadesh Barnea to see the land.
verses.explainer: He recalls: 'This is what your fathers did when I sent them from Kadesh-barnea to look at the land.'

verses.text: For when they went up to the valley of Eshcol, and saw the land, they discouraged the heart of the children of Israel, that they should not go into the land which Yahweh had given them.
verses.explainer: After the spies went to the Valley of Eshkol, they discouraged Israel from entering the land God had given them.

verses.text: Yahweh’s anger burned in that day, and he swore, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord's anger burned that day and he swore the oath against the first generation.

verses.text: ‘Surely none of the men who came up out of Egypt, from twenty years old and upward, shall see the land which I swore to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob; because they have not wholly followed me,
verses.explainer: None of the men twenty years or older who came from Egypt would see the land promised to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob -- because they did not follow God wholeheartedly.

verses.text: except Caleb the son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite, and Joshua the son of Nun; because they have followed Yahweh completely.’
verses.explainer: Except Caleb and Joshua, who followed the Lord wholeheartedly.

crossReferences.reference: Numbers 14:24 -- God singled out Caleb: 'Because my servant Caleb has a different spirit and follows me wholeheartedly, I will bring him into the land.'
crossReferences.connection: Numbers 14:24 -- God singled out Caleb: 'Because my servant Caleb has a different spirit and follows me wholeheartedly, I will bring him into the land.'

verses.text: Yahweh’s anger burned against Israel, and he made them wander back and forth in the wilderness forty years, until all the generation, who had done evil in Yahweh’s sight, was consumed.
verses.explainer: The Lord's anger burned against Israel and he made them wander forty years until the entire unfaithful generation had died.

verses.text: “Behold, you have risen up in your fathers’ place, an increase of sinful men, to increase the fierce anger of Yahweh toward Israel.
verses.explainer: Moses warns Reuben and Gad: 'Here you are, a brood of sinners, standing in the place of your fathers and making the Lord even more angry with Israel.'

verses.text: For if you turn away from after him, he will yet again leave them in the wilderness; and you will destroy all these people.”
verses.explainer: If they turn away from God, he will leave the people in the wilderness again, and they will be the cause of Israel's destruction.

verses.text: They came near to him, and said, “We will build sheepfolds here for our livestock, and cities for our little ones;
verses.explainer: They approach Moses with a counter-proposal: they will build pens for their livestock and cities for their families here.

verses.text: but we ourselves will be ready armed to go before the children of Israel, until we have brought them to their place. Our little ones shall dwell in the fortified cities because of the inhabitants of the land.
verses.explainer: But they will arm themselves and go ahead of the other Israelites until they have brought them to their place. Their families will stay in fortified cities for protection.

verses.text: We will not return to our houses, until the children of Israel have each inherited their inheritance.
verses.explainer: They will not return home until every Israelite has received their inheritance.

verses.text: For we will not inherit with them on the other side of the Jordan, and forward; because our inheritance has come to us on this side of the Jordan eastward.”
verses.explainer: They will not receive any inheritance west of the Jordan because their inheritance has come to them on the east side.

verses.text: Moses said to them, “If you will do this thing, if you will arm yourselves to go before Yahweh to the war,
verses.explainer: Moses agrees conditionally: if they arm themselves for battle before the Lord --

verses.text: and every armed man of you will pass over the Jordan before Yahweh, until he has driven out his enemies from before him,
verses.explainer: And every armed man crosses the Jordan before the Lord until he has driven out his enemies --

verses.text: and the land is subdued before Yahweh; then afterward you shall return, and be guiltless towards Yahweh, and towards Israel; and this land shall be to you for a possession before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: And the land is subdued before the Lord -- then they may return. They will be free from obligation to the Lord and to Israel, and this land will be their possession before the Lord.

verses.text: “But if you will not do so, behold, you have sinned against Yahweh; and be sure your sin will find you out.
verses.explainer: But Moses warns: 'If you fail to do this, you will be sinning against the Lord; and you may be sure that your sin will find you out.'

crossReferences.reference: Galatians 6:7 -- Paul echoes this principle: 'Do not be deceived: God cannot be mocked. A man reaps what he sows.'
crossReferences.connection: Galatians 6:7 -- Paul echoes this principle: 'Do not be deceived: God cannot be mocked. A man reaps what he sows.'

verses.text: Build cities for your little ones, and folds for your sheep; and do that which has proceeded out of your mouth.”
verses.explainer: Moses tells them: build cities for your families and pens for your flocks -- but do what you have promised.

verses.text: The children of Gad and the children of Reuben spoke to Moses, saying, “Your servants will do as my lord commands.
verses.explainer: The Gadites and Reubenites answer: 'We your servants will do as our lord commands.'

verses.text: Our little ones, our wives, our flocks, and all our livestock, shall be there in the cities of Gilead;
verses.explainer: Their children, wives, flocks, and herds will remain in the cities of Gilead.

verses.text: but your servants will pass over, every man who is armed for war, before Yahweh to battle, as my lord says.”
verses.explainer: But they will cross over, every man armed for battle, before the Lord to fight, just as their lord says.

verses.text: So Moses commanded concerning them to Eleazar the priest, and to Joshua the son of Nun, and to the heads of the fathers’ households of the tribes of the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: Moses gives orders to Eleazar, Joshua, and the tribal heads concerning them.

verses.text: Moses said to them, “If the children of Gad and the children of Reuben will pass with you over the Jordan, every man who is armed to battle, before Yahweh, and the land is subdued before you, then you shall give them the land of Gilead for a possession;
verses.explainer: If Gad and Reuben cross the Jordan armed and the land is subdued, give them Gilead as their possession.

verses.text: but if they will not pass over with you armed, they shall have possessions among you in the land of Canaan.”
verses.explainer: But if they do not cross over armed, they must accept a possession in Canaan with the rest.

verses.text: The children of Gad and the children of Reuben answered, saying, “As Yahweh has said to your servants, so will we do.
verses.explainer: Gad and Reuben answer: 'What the Lord has said to your servants, we will do.'

verses.text: We will pass over armed before Yahweh into the land of Canaan, and the possession of our inheritance shall remain with us beyond the Jordan.”
verses.explainer: They will cross over armed before the Lord into Canaan, and the property of their inheritance will be on this side of the Jordan.

verses.text: Moses gave to them, even to the children of Gad, and to the children of Reuben, and to the half-tribe of Manasseh the son of Joseph, the kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites, and the kingdom of Og king of Bashan, the land, according to its cities and borders, even the cities of the surrounding land.
verses.explainer: Moses gives the Gadites, Reubenites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh son of Joseph the kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites and the kingdom of Og king of Bashan.

verses.text: The children of Gad built Dibon, Ataroth, Aroer,
verses.explainer: The Gadites build Dibon, Ataroth, Aroer --

verses.text: Atrothshophan, Jazer, Jogbehah,
verses.explainer: Atroth-shophan, Jazer, Jogbehah --

verses.text: Beth Nimrah, and Beth Haran: fortified cities, and folds for sheep.
verses.explainer: Beth-nimrah and Beth-haran as fortified cities, and build pens for their flocks.

verses.text: The children of Reuben built Heshbon, Elealeh, Kiriathaim,
verses.explainer: The Reubenites build Heshbon, Elealeh, Kiriathaim --

verses.text: Nebo, and Baal Meon, (their names being changed), and Sibmah. They gave other names to the cities which they built.
verses.explainer: Nebo, Baal-meon (with changed names), and Sibmah. They rename the cities they rebuild.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Renaming conquered cities was standard practice in the ancient Near East -- it established new ownership and new identity. Cities like 'Baal-meon' had their pagan deity-names modified.

verses.text: The children of Machir the son of Manasseh went to Gilead, took it, and dispossessed the Amorites who were therein.
verses.explainer: The descendants of Makir son of Manasseh go to Gilead, capture it, and drive out the Amorites.

verses.text: Moses gave Gilead to Machir the son of Manasseh; and he lived therein.
verses.explainer: Moses gives Gilead to the Makirites (descendants of Manasseh), and they settle there.

verses.text: Jair the son of Manasseh went and took its villages, and called them Havvoth Jair.
verses.explainer: Jair, a descendant of Manasseh, captures their settlements and calls them Havvoth-jair ('settlements of Jair').

verses.text: Nobah went and took Kenath, and its villages, and called it Nobah, after his own name.
verses.explainer: Nobah captures Kenath and its surrounding settlements and calls it Nobah after himself.

thenVsNow.then: The Transjordan settlement became permanent, but these tribes were the first to fall to foreign invasion (the Assyrians in 733 BC), supporting the view that settling short of Canaan was spiritually risky.
thenVsNow.now: Settling for 'good enough' rather than pursuing God's full purpose is a recurring human tendency. Sometimes the practical choice proves to be the compromised one.

explainer.summary: Moses records Israel's journey from Egypt to the plains of Moab, listing forty-two camping sites along the way. This is the most complete itinerary in the Bible, documenting the entire route from Rameses in Egypt through the wilderness wanderings to the border of Canaan. The chapter concludes with God's command to drive out the inhabitants of Canaan and destroy their idols, with a warning that failure to do so will bring consequences.
themes: God's faithfulness through forty years of travel
The documented record of Israel's journey
Command to drive out Canaan's inhabitants
explainer.contextInBook: Before Israel enters the land, Moses looks backward over the entire journey. The itinerary serves as a historical record and a theological testimony: God brought them through every station, every wilderness, every challenge. The past faithfulness of God is the basis for trusting him with the future.

wordStudies.significance: Each 'massa' (from 'nasa,' to pull up stakes) represents a campsite where Israel pitched and then departed. The forty-two stations parallel the number of generations in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus, a connection some interpreters find significant.

verses.text: These are the journeys of the children of Israel, when they went out of the land of Egypt by their armies under the hand of Moses and Aaron.
verses.explainer: These are the stages of Israel's journey when they came out of Egypt by divisions under the leadership of Moses and Aaron.

verses.text: Moses wrote their goings out according to their journeys by the commandment of Yahweh. These are their journeys according to their goings out.
verses.explainer: At the Lord's command Moses recorded the stages of their journey. These are the stages listed by their departure points.

verses.text: They traveled from Rameses in the first month, on the fifteenth day of the first month; on the next day after the Passover, the children of Israel went out with a high hand in the sight of all the Egyptians,
verses.explainer: They departed from Rameses on the fifteenth day of the first month, the day after the Passover. They marched out defiantly in full view of all the Egyptians.

crossReferences.reference: Exodus 12:37 -- The departure from Rameses on the morning after the Passover is the foundation event of Israel's national history.
crossReferences.connection: Exodus 12:37 -- The departure from Rameses on the morning after the Passover is the foundation event of Israel's national history.

verses.text: while the Egyptians were burying all their firstborn, whom Yahweh had struck among them. Yahweh also executed judgments on their gods.
verses.explainer: The Egyptians were burying their firstborn whom the Lord had struck down -- God had brought judgment on their gods.

verses.text: The children of Israel traveled from Rameses, and encamped in Succoth.
verses.explainer: Israel left Rameses and camped at Sukkoth.

verses.text: They traveled from Succoth, and encamped in Etham, which is in the edge of the wilderness.
verses.explainer: They left Sukkoth and camped at Etham, on the edge of the wilderness.

verses.text: They traveled from Etham, and turned back to Pihahiroth, which is before Baal Zephon: and they encamped before Migdol.
verses.explainer: They left Etham, turned back to Pi-hahiroth east of Baal-zephon, and camped near Migdol.

verses.text: They traveled from before Hahiroth, and crossed through the middle of the sea into the wilderness. They went three days’ journey in the wilderness of Etham, and encamped in Marah.
verses.explainer: They left Pi-hahiroth and passed through the sea into the wilderness. They traveled three days in the Desert of Etham and camped at Marah.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The sea crossing is mentioned almost matter-of-factly in this itinerary -- just another station on the journey. What was the most dramatic moment of the Exodus becomes a single line in the travel log, showing that the miraculous had become part of Israel's routine experience.

verses.text: They traveled from Marah, and came to Elim. In Elim, there were twelve springs of water, and seventy palm trees; and they encamped there.
verses.explainer: They left Marah and went to Elim, where there were twelve springs and seventy palm trees, and they camped there.

verses.text: They traveled from Elim, and encamped by the Red Sea.
verses.explainer: They left Elim and camped by the Red Sea.

verses.text: They traveled from the Red Sea, and encamped in the wilderness of Sin.
verses.explainer: They left the Red Sea and camped in the Desert of Sin.

verses.text: They traveled from the wilderness of Sin, and encamped in Dophkah.
verses.explainer: They left the Desert of Sin and camped at Dophkah.

verses.text: They traveled from Dophkah, and encamped in Alush.
verses.explainer: They left Dophkah and camped at Alush.

verses.text: They traveled from Alush, and encamped in Rephidim, where there was no water for the people to drink.
verses.explainer: They left Alush and camped at Rephidim, where there was no water for the people to drink.

crossReferences.reference: Exodus 17:1-7 -- At Rephidim, Moses struck the rock to bring water, and Israel fought the Amalekites. Two major events occurred at this single campsite.
crossReferences.connection: Exodus 17:1-7 -- At Rephidim, Moses struck the rock to bring water, and Israel fought the Amalekites. Two major events occurred at this single campsite.

verses.text: They traveled from Rephidim, and encamped in the wilderness of Sinai.
verses.explainer: They left Rephidim and camped in the Desert of Sinai.

verses.text: They traveled from the wilderness of Sinai, and encamped in Kibroth Hattaavah.
verses.explainer: They left the Desert of Sinai and camped at Kibroth-hattaavah.

thenVsNow.then: Kibroth-hattaavah ('Graves of Craving') was where God sent quail and then struck those who craved meat. The name preserved the memory of judgment as a warning.
thenVsNow.now: Place names can serve as permanent reminders of both blessing and judgment. Every community has its 'Kibroth-hattaavah' -- places where desire led to disaster.

verses.text: They traveled from Kibroth Hattaavah, and encamped in Hazeroth.
verses.explainer: They left Kibroth-hattaavah and camped at Hazeroth.

verses.text: They traveled from Hazeroth, and encamped in Rithmah.
verses.explainer: They left Hazeroth and camped at Rithmah.

verses.text: They traveled from Rithmah, and encamped in Rimmon Perez.
verses.explainer: They left Rithmah and camped at Rimmon-perez.

verses.text: They traveled from Rimmon Perez, and encamped in Libnah.
verses.explainer: They left Rimmon-perez and camped at Libnah.

verses.text: They traveled from Libnah, and encamped in Rissah.
verses.explainer: They left Libnah and camped at Rissah.

verses.text: They traveled from Rissah, and encamped in Kehelathah.
verses.explainer: They left Rissah and camped at Kehelathah.

verses.text: They traveled from Kehelathah, and encamped in Mount Shepher.
verses.explainer: They left Kehelathah and camped at Mount Shepher.

verses.text: They traveled from Mount Shepher, and encamped in Haradah.
verses.explainer: They left Mount Shepher and camped at Haradah.

verses.text: They traveled from Haradah, and encamped in Makheloth.
verses.explainer: They left Haradah and camped at Makheloth.

verses.text: They traveled from Makheloth, and encamped in Tahath.
verses.explainer: They left Makheloth and camped at Tahath.

verses.text: They traveled from Tahath, and encamped in Terah.
verses.explainer: They left Tahath and camped at Terah.

verses.text: They traveled from Terah, and encamped in Mithkah.
verses.explainer: They left Terah and camped at Mithkah.

verses.text: They traveled from Mithkah, and encamped in Hashmonah.
verses.explainer: They left Mithkah and camped at Hashmonah.

verses.text: They traveled from Hashmonah, and encamped in Moseroth.
verses.explainer: They left Hashmonah and camped at Moseroth.

verses.text: They traveled from Moseroth, and encamped in Bene Jaakan.
verses.explainer: They left Moseroth and camped at Bene-jaakan.

verses.text: They traveled from Bene Jaakan, and encamped in Hor Haggidgad.
verses.explainer: They left Bene-jaakan and camped at Hor-haggidgad.

verses.text: They traveled from Hor Haggidgad, and encamped in Jotbathah.
verses.explainer: They left Hor-haggidgad and camped at Jotbathah.

verses.text: They traveled from Jotbathah, and encamped in Abronah.
verses.explainer: They left Jotbathah and camped at Abronah.

verses.text: They traveled from Abronah, and encamped in Ezion Geber.
verses.explainer: They left Abronah and camped at Ezion-geber.

verses.text: They traveled from Ezion Geber, and encamped at Kadesh in the wilderness of Zin.
verses.explainer: They left Ezion-geber and camped at Kadesh, in the Desert of Zin.

verses.text: They traveled from Kadesh, and encamped in Mount Hor, in the edge of the land of Edom.
verses.explainer: They left Kadesh and camped at Mount Hor, on the border of Edom.

verses.text: Aaron the priest went up into Mount Hor at the commandment of Yahweh, and died there, in the fortieth year after the children of Israel had come out of the land of Egypt, in the fifth month, on the first day of the month.
verses.explainer: Aaron the priest went up Mount Hor at the Lord's command and died there, on the first day of the fifth month of the fortieth year after the Israelites came out of Egypt.

verses.text: Aaron was one hundred twenty-three years old when he died in Mount Hor.
verses.explainer: Aaron was 123 years old when he died on Mount Hor.

verses.text: The Canaanite, the king of Arad, who lived in the South in the land of Canaan, heard of the coming of the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: The Canaanite king of Arad, who lived in the Negev of Canaan, heard that the Israelites were coming.

verses.text: They traveled from Mount Hor, and encamped in Zalmonah.
verses.explainer: They left Mount Hor and camped at Zalmonah.

verses.text: They traveled from Zalmonah, and encamped in Punon.
verses.explainer: They left Zalmonah and camped at Punon.

verses.text: They traveled from Punon, and encamped in Oboth.
verses.explainer: They left Punon and camped at Oboth.

verses.text: They traveled from Oboth, and encamped in Iye Abarim, in the border of Moab.
verses.explainer: They left Oboth and camped at Iye-abarim, on the border of Moab.

verses.text: They traveled from Iyim, and encamped in Dibon Gad.
verses.explainer: They left Iye-abarim and camped at Dibon-gad.

verses.text: They traveled from Dibon Gad, and encamped in Almon Diblathaim.
verses.explainer: They left Dibon-gad and camped at Almon-diblathaim.

verses.text: They traveled from Almon Diblathaim, and encamped in the mountains of Abarim, before Nebo.
verses.explainer: They left Almon-diblathaim and camped in the mountains of Abarim, near Nebo.

verses.text: They traveled from the mountains of Abarim, and encamped in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho.
verses.explainer: They left the mountains of Abarim and camped on the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho.

thenVsNow.then: The final campsite is the staging area for invasion. Forty-two stations after Rameses, Israel stands on the threshold of the Promised Land.
thenVsNow.now: Looking back over a long journey reveals God's faithfulness at every stage. The final station validates every difficult step that preceded it.

verses.text: They encamped by the Jordan, from Beth Jeshimoth even to Abel Shittim in the plains of Moab.
verses.explainer: They camped along the Jordan from Beth-jeshimoth to Abel-shittim on the plains of Moab.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho, saying,
verses.explainer: On the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho, the Lord speaks to Moses.

verses.text: Speak to the children of Israel, and tell them, “When you pass over the Jordan into the land of Canaan,
verses.explainer: Tell the Israelites: when you cross the Jordan into Canaan --

verses.text: then you shall drive out all the inhabitants of the land from before you, destroy all their stone idols, destroy all their molten images, and demolish all their high places.
verses.explainer: Drive out all the inhabitants of the land before you. Destroy all their carved images and cast idols, and demolish all their high places.

verses.text: You shall take possession of the land, and dwell therein; for I have given the land to you to possess it.
verses.explainer: Take possession of the land and settle in it, for I have given you the land to possess.

verses.text: You shall inherit the land by lot according to your families; to the more you shall give the more inheritance, and to the fewer you shall give the less inheritance. Wherever the lot falls to any man, that shall be his. You shall inherit according to the tribes of your fathers.
verses.explainer: Distribute the land by lot according to your clans. Give a larger inheritance to larger groups and a smaller one to smaller groups.

verses.text: “But if you do not drive out the inhabitants of the land from before you, then those you let remain of them will be as pricks in your eyes and as thorns in your sides, and they will harass you in the land in which you dwell.
verses.explainer: If you do not drive out the inhabitants, those who remain will become barbs in your eyes and thorns in your sides. They will give you trouble in the land where you live.

crossReferences.reference: Joshua 23:12-13 -- Joshua repeats this warning near the end of his life, and the book of Judges demonstrates its fulfillment when Israel fails to drive out the Canaanites.
crossReferences.connection: Joshua 23:12-13 -- Joshua repeats this warning near the end of his life, and the book of Judges demonstrates its fulfillment when Israel fails to drive out the Canaanites.

verses.text: It shall happen that as I thought to do to them, so will I do to you.”
verses.explainer: And I will do to you what I planned to do to them.

thenVsNow.then: The warning is stark: the judgment intended for the Canaanites will fall on Israel if they adopt Canaanite practices. The same land that vomits out the Canaanites will vomit out Israel for the same sins.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that God's people face the same standards as everyone else -- indeed higher ones -- runs throughout Scripture. Privilege increases accountability.

explainer.summary: God defines the precise boundaries of the Promised Land that the nine and a half tribes will inherit west of the Jordan. The southern boundary runs from the Dead Sea through the Desert of Zin to the Brook of Egypt. The western boundary is the Mediterranean Sea. The northern boundary extends from the coast to Mount Hor (in Lebanon), Lebo-hamath, and east to Zedad. The eastern boundary runs south to the Sea of Kinnereth (Galilee) and down the Jordan to the Dead Sea. God also appoints tribal leaders to oversee the land distribution.
themes: The land as covenant promise fulfilled
explainer.contextInBook: After the travel itinerary (chapter 33), God defines the destination. The boundaries make concrete what has been promised abstractly since Abraham. Israel will not wander indefinitely -- they have a specific, bounded homeland.

wordStudies.significance: The word 'gevul' appears repeatedly, emphasizing that the Promised Land is not unlimited but has specific, God-defined borders. The boundaries serve both practical and theological purposes: they define Israel's responsibility and limit their ambition.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the boundaries of Canaan as it is allotted to Israel.

verses.text: “Command the children of Israel, and tell them, ‘When you come into the land of Canaan (this is the land that shall fall to you for an inheritance, even the land of Canaan according to its borders),
verses.explainer: God commands: when you enter Canaan, this is the land that will be assigned to you, with its boundaries.

verses.text: then your south quarter shall be from the wilderness of Zin along by the side of Edom, and your south border shall be from the end of the Salt Sea eastward.
verses.explainer: The southern boundary starts from the Desert of Zin along the border of Edom, running east from the southern end of the Dead Sea.

verses.text: Your border shall turn about southward of the ascent of Akrabbim, and pass along to Zin; and it shall pass southward of Kadesh Barnea; and it shall go from there to Hazar Addar, and pass along to Azmon.
verses.explainer: The boundary turns south to the Ascent of Akrabbim, continues through Zin, and reaches south of Kadesh-barnea, extending to Hazar-addar and on to Azmon.

verses.text: The border shall turn about from Azmon to the brook of Egypt, and it shall end at the sea.
verses.explainer: From Azmon the boundary turns to the Wadi of Egypt and ends at the Mediterranean Sea.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The 'Wadi of Egypt' (Nahal Mitsrayim) is not the Nile but the Wadi el-Arish, a seasonal watercourse in northern Sinai that marked the traditional boundary between Canaan and Egypt.

verses.text: “‘For the western border, you shall have the great sea and its border. This shall be your west border.
verses.explainer: The western boundary is the Mediterranean Sea -- the Great Sea and its coastline.

verses.text: “‘This shall be your north border: from the great sea you shall mark out for yourselves Mount Hor.
verses.explainer: The northern boundary runs from the Mediterranean to Mount Hor -- not the same Mount Hor where Aaron died, but a peak in the Lebanon range.

verses.text: From Mount Hor you shall mark out to the entrance of Hamath; and the border shall pass by Zedad.
verses.explainer: From Mount Hor the boundary extends to Lebo-hamath (the entrance of Hamath).

verses.text: Then the border shall go to Ziphron, and it shall end at Hazar Enan. This shall be your north border.
verses.explainer: The boundary continues to Zedad.

verses.text: “‘You shall mark out your east border from Hazar Enan to Shepham.
verses.explainer: The eastern boundary runs from Hazar-enan to Shepham.

verses.text: The border shall go down from Shepham to Riblah, on the east side of Ain. The border shall go down, and shall reach to the side of the sea of Chinnereth eastward.
verses.explainer: From Shepham the boundary goes down to Riblah on the east side of Ain, continues along the slopes east of the Sea of Kinnereth (Sea of Galilee).

crossReferences.reference: Luke 5:1 -- The Sea of Kinnereth (Galilee) became the center of Jesus' Galilean ministry. The boundary of the Promised Land passed alongside the lake where Jesus would call his first disciples.
crossReferences.connection: Luke 5:1 -- The Sea of Kinnereth (Galilee) became the center of Jesus' Galilean ministry. The boundary of the Promised Land passed alongside the lake where Jesus would call his first disciples.

verses.text: The border shall go down to the Jordan, and end at the Salt Sea. This shall be your land according to its borders around it.’”
verses.explainer: The boundary continues down the Jordan to the Dead Sea. This will be your land, defined by its boundaries on every side.

thenVsNow.then: The defined boundaries excluded the Transjordan territory settled by Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh -- technically, their land was outside the 'Promised Land' proper.
thenVsNow.now: Israel never fully occupied the entire territory described here. The largest extent was reached under David and Solomon, but even then the boundaries were approximate rather than exact.

verses.text: Moses commanded the children of Israel, saying, “This is the land which you shall inherit by lot, which Yahweh has commanded to give to the nine tribes, and to the half-tribe;
verses.explainer: Moses commands the Israelites: this is the land you are to receive by lot. The Lord has ordered that it be given to the nine and a half tribes.

verses.text: for the tribe of the children of Reuben according to their fathers’ houses, and the tribe of the children of Gad according to their fathers’ houses, have received, and the half-tribe of Manasseh have received, their inheritance.
verses.explainer: The tribes of Reuben and Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh have already received their inheritance east of the Jordan.

verses.text: The two tribes and the half-tribe have received their inheritance beyond the Jordan at Jericho eastward, toward the sunrise.”
verses.explainer: These two and a half tribes have received their inheritance on the east side of the Jordan across from Jericho, toward the sunrise.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord tells Moses which leaders will assign the land.

verses.text: “These are the names of the men who shall divide the land to you for inheritance: Eleazar the priest, and Joshua the son of Nun.
verses.explainer: Eleazar the priest and Joshua son of Nun will distribute the land.

verses.text: You shall take one prince of every tribe, to divide the land for inheritance.
verses.explainer: Appoint one leader from each tribe to help distribute the land.

verses.text: These are the names of the men: Of the tribe of Judah, Caleb the son of Jephunneh.
verses.explainer: From Judah: Caleb son of Jephunneh -- the faithful spy now receives his reward.

crossReferences.reference: Joshua 14:6-15 -- Caleb will personally request and receive Hebron, the territory he had scouted forty-five years earlier.
crossReferences.connection: Joshua 14:6-15 -- Caleb will personally request and receive Hebron, the territory he had scouted forty-five years earlier.

verses.text: Of the tribe of the children of Simeon, Shemuel the son of Ammihud.
verses.explainer: From Simeon: Shemuel son of Ammihud.

verses.text: Of the tribe of Benjamin, Elidad the son of Chislon.
verses.explainer: From Benjamin: Elidad son of Kislon.

verses.text: Of the tribe of the children of Dan a prince, Bukki the son of Jogli.
verses.explainer: From Dan: a leader, Bukki son of Jogli.

verses.text: Of the children of Joseph: of the tribe of the children of Manasseh a prince, Hanniel the son of Ephod.
verses.explainer: From the sons of Joseph, for Manasseh: Hanniel son of Ephod.

verses.text: Of the tribe of the children of Ephraim a prince, Kemuel the son of Shiphtan.
verses.explainer: From Ephraim: Kemuel son of Shiphtan.

verses.text: Of the tribe of the children of Zebulun a prince, Elizaphan the son of Parnach.
verses.explainer: From Zebulun: Elizaphan son of Parnach.

verses.text: Of the tribe of the children of Issachar a prince, Paltiel the son of Azzan.
verses.explainer: From Issachar: Paltiel son of Azzan.

verses.text: Of the tribe of the children of Asher a prince, Ahihud the son of Shelomi.
verses.explainer: From Asher: Ahihud son of Shelomi.

verses.text: Of the tribe of the children of Naphtali a prince, Pedahel the son of Ammihud.”
verses.explainer: From Naphtali: Pedahel son of Ammihud.

verses.text: These are they whom Yahweh commanded to divide the inheritance to the children of Israel in the land of Canaan.
verses.explainer: These are the ones the Lord commanded to assign the inheritance to the Israelites in the land of Canaan.

explainer.summary: God commands that forty-eight cities be given to the Levites throughout the land, since they receive no tribal territory. Six of these cities are designated as cities of refuge -- three on each side of the Jordan -- where someone who kills another person accidentally can flee from the 'avenger of blood.' The chapter establishes detailed legal distinctions between murder and manslaughter, and specifies that the accused must remain in the city of refuge until the death of the high priest.
themes: Provision for the Levites without tribal land
Cities of refuge as sanctuaries for the accused
Legal distinction between murder and manslaughter
The avenger of blood and restorative justice
The high priest's death as release
explainer.contextInBook: The cities of refuge address a critical need for the coming settlement: how to prevent blood feuds from tearing apart the community. Without a mechanism for distinguishing accidental killing from intentional murder, the land would be consumed by cycles of revenge.

wordStudies.significance: The 'goel haddam' was the nearest male relative of a murder victim, obligated to pursue and execute the killer. The city of refuge system channeled this obligation through legal process rather than eliminating it, balancing justice with protection for the innocent.

The cities of 'miklat' provided legal sanctuary. They were not about escaping justice but ensuring that justice was properly administered. The accused could live safely until trial and, if found innocent of intentional murder, could remain until the high priest's death.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho, saying,
verses.explainer: On the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho, the Lord speaks to Moses.

verses.text: “Command the children of Israel to give to the Levites of the inheritance of their possession cities to dwell in. You shall give suburbs for the cities around them to the Levites.
verses.explainer: Command the Israelites to give the Levites towns to live in from the inheritance the Israelites will possess, and pasturelands around those towns.

verses.text: They shall have the cities to dwell in. Their suburbs shall be for their livestock, and for their possessions, and for all their animals.
verses.explainer: The Levites will have towns to live in and pasturelands for their cattle, flocks, and all their other livestock.

verses.text: “The suburbs of the cities, which you shall give to the Levites, shall be from the wall of the city and outward one thousand cubits around it.
verses.explainer: The pasturelands extend one thousand cubits from the town wall on every side.

verses.text: You shall measure outside of the city for the east side two thousand cubits, and for the south side two thousand cubits, and for the west side two thousand cubits, and for the north side two thousand cubits, the city being in the middle. This shall be the suburbs of their cities.
verses.explainer: Outside the town, measure two thousand cubits on the east, south, west, and north sides, with the town in the center. This will be their pasturelands for their towns.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The specified measurements created a suburban zone around each Levitical city -- enough land for gardens and livestock but not large farms. The Levites lived modestly among the people they served.

verses.text: “The cities which you shall give to the Levites, they shall be the six cities of refuge, which you shall give for the man slayer to flee to. Besides them you shall give forty-two cities.
verses.explainer: Six of the towns given to the Levites will be cities of refuge, where someone who has killed a person may flee. In addition, give them forty-two other towns.

verses.text: All the cities which you shall give to the Levites shall be forty-eight cities together with their suburbs.
verses.explainer: The total number of towns for the Levites: forty-eight, together with their pasturelands.

verses.text: Concerning the cities which you shall give of the possession of the children of Israel, from the many you shall take many; and from the few you shall take few. Everyone according to his inheritance which he inherits shall give some of his cities to the Levites.”
verses.explainer: The towns given from each tribe will be proportional -- more from larger tribes, fewer from smaller ones.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the cities of refuge.

verses.text: “Speak to the children of Israel, and tell them, ‘When you pass over the Jordan into the land of Canaan,
verses.explainer: Tell the Israelites: when you cross the Jordan into Canaan, select cities of refuge.

verses.text: then you shall appoint for yourselves cities to be cities of refuge for you, that the man slayer who kills any person unwittingly may flee there.
verses.explainer: These cities will be a place of refuge where someone who has killed another person accidentally may flee.

thenVsNow.then: Without cities of refuge, accidental killers faced immediate execution by the victim's family. The cities provided a legal process between the killing and the verdict.
thenVsNow.now: The cities of refuge represent one of the earliest codified legal protections for the accused -- the principle that even alleged killers deserve a hearing before punishment.

verses.text: The cities shall be to you for refuge from the avenger, that the man slayer not die, until he stands before the congregation for judgment.
verses.explainer: The cities will be refuge from the avenger of blood, so that anyone accused of murder will not die before standing trial before the assembly.

verses.text: The cities which you shall give shall be for you six cities of refuge.
verses.explainer: Designate six cities of refuge.

verses.text: You shall give three cities beyond the Jordan, and you shall give three cities in the land of Canaan. They shall be cities of refuge.
verses.explainer: Three east of the Jordan and three in Canaan will be cities of refuge.

crossReferences.reference: Deuteronomy 19:1-13 -- Moses expands on the cities of refuge, including the provision that roads to them should be maintained so that a fugitive can reach one quickly.
crossReferences.connection: Deuteronomy 19:1-13 -- Moses expands on the cities of refuge, including the provision that roads to them should be maintained so that a fugitive can reach one quickly.

verses.text: For the children of Israel, and for the stranger and for the foreigner living among them, shall these six cities be for refuge; that everyone who kills any person unwittingly may flee there.
verses.explainer: These six cities are a place of refuge for Israelites, for foreigners, and for any other people living among them, so that anyone who kills a person accidentally can flee there.

verses.text: “‘But if he struck him with an instrument of iron, so that he died, he is a murderer. The murderer shall surely be put to death.
verses.explainer: If someone strikes a person with an iron object and that person dies, the killer is a murderer. The murderer is to be put to death.

verses.text: If he struck him with a stone in the hand, by which a man may die, and he died, he is a murderer. The murderer shall surely be put to death.
verses.explainer: If someone strikes a person with a stone large enough to kill, and that person dies, the killer is a murderer.

verses.text: Or if he struck him with a weapon of wood in the hand, by which a man may die, and he died, he is a murderer. The murderer shall surely be put to death.
verses.explainer: If someone strikes a person with a wooden object large enough to kill, and that person dies, the killer is a murderer.

verses.text: The avenger of blood shall himself put the murderer to death. When he meets him, he shall put him to death.
verses.explainer: The avenger of blood shall put the murderer to death. When the avenger finds the murderer, the avenger shall put the murderer to death.

verses.text: If he shoved him out of hatred, or hurled at him, lying in wait, so that he died,
verses.explainer: If anyone with malice aforethought pushes another person or throws something at them intentionally so that they die --

verses.text: or in hostility struck him with his hand, so that he died, he who struck him shall surely be put to death. He is a murderer. The avenger of blood shall put the murderer to death, when he meets him.
verses.explainer: Or if in hostility someone hits another with a fist so that the other person dies, the attacker shall be put to death as a murderer. The avenger of blood shall put the murderer to death when they meet.

verses.text: “‘But if he shoved him suddenly without hostility, or hurled on him anything without lying in wait,
verses.explainer: But if without hostility someone suddenly pushes another or throws something at them unintentionally --

verses.text: or with any stone, by which a man may die, not seeing him, and cast it on him, so that he died, and he was not his enemy, neither sought his harm;
verses.explainer: Or drops a stone on them without seeing them, and they die -- if they were not enemies and no harm was intended --

verses.text: then the congregation shall judge between the striker and the avenger of blood according to these ordinances.
verses.explainer: The assembly must judge between the accused and the avenger of blood according to these regulations.

verses.text: The congregation shall deliver the man slayer out of the hand of the avenger of blood, and the congregation shall restore him to his city of refuge, where he had fled. He shall dwell therein until the death of the high priest, who was anointed with the holy oil.
verses.explainer: The assembly must protect the one accused of manslaughter from the avenger and return them to the city of refuge where they fled. They must stay there until the death of the high priest.

crossReferences.reference: Hebrews 9:15 -- Christ is the mediator of a new covenant whose death sets people free -- echoing the release that came when the high priest died.
crossReferences.connection: Hebrews 9:15 -- Christ is the mediator of a new covenant whose death sets people free -- echoing the release that came when the high priest died.

verses.text: “‘But if the man slayer shall at any time go beyond the border of his city of refuge, where he flees,
verses.explainer: If the accused ever goes outside the limits of the city of refuge --

verses.text: and the avenger of blood finds him outside of the border of his city of refuge, and the avenger of blood kills the man slayer; he shall not be guilty of blood,
verses.explainer: And the avenger of blood finds them outside the city, the avenger may kill the accused without being guilty of murder.

thenVsNow.then: The city of refuge provided protection only within its limits. Leaving meant accepting the risk of death at the avenger's hand.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that sanctuary has boundaries persists in legal systems. Asylum is conditional on remaining within the protecting jurisdiction.

verses.text: because he should have remained in his city of refuge until the death of the high priest. But after the death of the high priest, the man slayer shall return into the land of his possession.
verses.explainer: The accused must stay in the city of refuge until the death of the high priest. Only after the high priest's death may they return to their own property.

verses.text: “‘These things shall be for a statute and ordinance to you throughout your generations in all your dwellings.
verses.explainer: These are to be lasting ordinances for you throughout your generations, wherever you live.

verses.text: “‘Whoever kills any person, the murderer shall be slain at the mouth of witnesses; but one witness shall not testify against any person that he die.
verses.explainer: Anyone who kills a person is to be put to death as a murderer only on the testimony of witnesses. No one is to be put to death on the testimony of only one witness.

crossReferences.reference: Deuteronomy 17:6 -- The two-witness requirement for capital cases became foundational to Jewish legal procedure and was cited by Jesus (Matthew 18:16) and Paul (2 Corinthians 13:1).
crossReferences.connection: Deuteronomy 17:6 -- The two-witness requirement for capital cases became foundational to Jewish legal procedure and was cited by Jesus (Matthew 18:16) and Paul (2 Corinthians 13:1).

verses.text: “‘Moreover you shall take no ransom for the life of a murderer who is guilty of death; but he shall surely be put to death.
verses.explainer: Do not accept a ransom for the life of a murderer who deserves to die. They are to be put to death.

verses.text: “‘You shall take no ransom for him who is fled to his city of refuge, that he may come again to dwell in the land, until the death of the priest.
verses.explainer: Do not accept a ransom for anyone who has fled to a city of refuge, allowing them to return before the death of the high priest.

verses.text: “‘So you shall not pollute the land in which you are; for blood pollutes the land. No atonement can be made for the land for the blood that is shed in it, but by the blood of him who shed it.
verses.explainer: Do not pollute the land where you are. Bloodshed pollutes the land, and atonement cannot be made for the land on which blood has been shed, except by the blood of the one who shed it.

verses.text: You shall not defile the land which you inhabit, in the middle of which I dwell; for I, Yahweh, dwell in the middle of the children of Israel.’”
verses.explainer: Do not defile the land where you live and where I dwell, for I, the Lord, dwell among the Israelites.

thenVsNow.then: Unpunished murder literally 'pollutes' the land where God dwells. The theological geography is clear: God's presence in the land requires that bloodguilt be properly addressed.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that unaddressed injustice corrupts entire communities remains relevant. Systemic tolerance of violence poisons the social environment.

explainer.summary: Tribal leaders from Manasseh raise a concern about the ruling for Zelophehad's daughters (chapter 27): if these women marry men from other tribes, their inherited land will transfer to those tribes permanently, since Jubilee does not reverse tribal land transfers. God resolves the issue by requiring inheriting daughters to marry within their own tribe. Zelophehad's daughters comply, marrying their cousins. The book ends with a summary statement about the laws given on the plains of Moab.
themes: Balancing women's rights with tribal integrity
The book concludes looking toward the land
explainer.contextInBook: Numbers ends where it began -- with tribal organization. The first chapters organized the tribes for marching; the last chapter organizes them for settlement. The concern about land staying within tribes shows that the people are now genuinely planning for life in the Promised Land, a dramatic change from the generation that refused to enter.

wordStudies.significance: The Jubilee year (every fiftieth year) returned land to its original tribal owners. But if a woman inherited land and married into another tribe, the land would become that tribe's permanent property, since it was acquired by inheritance, not purchase. The Jubilee system could not correct this type of transfer.

verses.text: The heads of the fathers’ households of the family of the children of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh, of the families of the sons of Joseph, came near, and spoke before Moses, and before the princes, the heads of the fathers’ households of the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: The family heads of the clan of Gilead (from Manasseh, descendant of Joseph) present their concern to Moses and the leaders.

verses.text: They said, “Yahweh commanded my lord to give the land for inheritance by lot to the children of Israel. My lord was commanded by Yahweh to give the inheritance of Zelophehad our brother to his daughters.
verses.explainer: They remind Moses that the Lord commanded him to distribute the land by lot and to give Zelophehad's inheritance to his daughters.

verses.text: If they are married to any of the sons of the other tribes of the children of Israel, then will their inheritance be taken away from the inheritance of our fathers, and will be added to the inheritance of the tribe to which they shall belong. So will it be taken away from the lot of our inheritance.
verses.explainer: The concern: if the daughters marry men from other tribes, their inheritance will be taken from their ancestral tribal allotment and added to the tribe they marry into.

verses.text: When the jubilee of the children of Israel shall be, then will their inheritance be added to the inheritance of the tribe to which they shall belong. So their inheritance will be taken away from the inheritance of the tribe of our fathers.”
verses.explainer: Even during the Jubilee, the land would remain with the new tribe, permanently reducing Manasseh's allotment.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Jubilee provision (Leviticus 25) returned purchased land to the original owner's tribe. But inherited land that transferred through marriage was not a sale -- it was a permanent shift of tribal territory. The concern was legally sound.

verses.text: Moses commanded the children of Israel according to Yahweh’s word, saying, “The tribe of the sons of Joseph speaks right.
verses.explainer: Moses delivers the Lord's ruling: the tribe of Joseph's descendants is right in their concern.

thenVsNow.then: God affirms the concern as legitimate without reversing the daughters' inheritance right. The solution preserves both the women's rights and the tribal structure.
thenVsNow.now: The willingness to refine legal rulings when practical problems emerge demonstrates that law must be living and responsive. Precedent-setting cases create new questions that require additional jurisprudence.

verses.text: This is the thing which Yahweh does command concerning the daughters of Zelophehad, saying, ‘Let them be married to whom they think best; only they shall marry into the family of the tribe of their father.
verses.explainer: The Lord's command concerning Zelophehad's daughters: they may marry anyone they please, as long as they marry within their father's tribal clan.

verses.text: So shall no inheritance of the children of Israel move from tribe to tribe; for the children of Israel shall all keep the inheritance of the tribe of his fathers.
verses.explainer: No inheritance in Israel is to pass from one tribe to another -- every Israelite is to keep the tribal inheritance of their ancestors.

verses.text: Every daughter who possesses an inheritance in any tribe of the children of Israel shall be wife to one of the family of the tribe of her father, that the children of Israel may each possess the inheritance of his fathers.
verses.explainer: Every daughter who inherits land in any tribe must marry someone within her father's tribal clan.

verses.text: So shall no inheritance move from one tribe to another tribe; for the tribes of the children of Israel shall each keep his own inheritance.’”
verses.explainer: No inheritance may pass from one tribe to another, for each tribe of Israel shall keep its own inheritance.

verses.text: The daughters of Zelophehad did as Yahweh commanded Moses:
verses.explainer: Zelophehad's daughters do as the Lord commanded Moses.

verses.text: for Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Noah, the daughters of Zelophehad, were married to their father’s brothers’ sons.
verses.explainer: Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Noah -- Zelophehad's daughters -- marry their cousins on their father's side.

verses.text: They were married into the families of the sons of Manasseh the son of Joseph. Their inheritance remained in the tribe of the family of their father.
verses.explainer: They marry within the clans of the descendants of Manasseh son of Joseph, and their inheritance remains in their father's clan and tribe.

verses.text: These are the commandments and the ordinances which Yahweh commanded by Moses to the children of Israel in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho.
verses.explainer: These are the commands and regulations the Lord gave through Moses to the Israelites on the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho.

thenVsNow.then: The book ends on the plains of Moab, looking toward Jericho and the Promised Land. After forty years, Israel stands where they should have been at the beginning -- but now with a new generation ready to enter.
thenVsNow.now: The ending of Numbers is a beginning. Everything points forward to what comes next -- Deuteronomy's farewell speeches and Joshua's conquest. The wilderness is behind; the land is ahead.
